activities.text: Gardens of the Prince (Jardín del Príncipe)
activities.detail: Wander through the vast landscaped riverside gardens dotted with fountains, sculptures, and centuries-old trees.
See the collection of ornate royal barges once used for leisure trips along the Tagus River.
Visit this small but extravagantly decorated royal retreat showcasing Neoclassical opulence in miniature.

activities.text: Stroll the Calle de la Reina
activities.detail: Walk this elegant tree-lined avenue connecting the palace to the town, popular with locals for evening walks.

activities.text: Explore the Plaza de Parejas and Square of San Antonio
activities.detail: Admire the symmetrical 18th-century urban planning and grand architecture near the palace entrance.

activities.text: Boat or train ride on the Strawberry Train (Tren de la Fresa)
activities.detail: Take the historic steam train from Madrid, staffed by attendants in period costume handing out fresh strawberries.

food.detail: Sample the region's famous strawberries and large fresones, sold fresh by roadside stalls and in local desserts.
Try the prized local white and green asparagus, often served simply grilled or with a light vinaigrette.
A hearty vegetable stew made with produce from the fertile orchards along the Tagus River.
Green beans sautéed with cured Spanish ham, a classic regional side dish.
A local twist on Spain's classic potato omelette, incorporating the town's celebrated asparagus.

culture.detail: Aranjuez is an easy 45-minute train ride from Madrid, making it a popular weekend excursion for locals and tourists alike.

culture.text: Visit in spring for peak produce
culture.detail: Strawberries and asparagus are harvested in spring, so markets and stalls are liveliest during this season.
Aranjuez is recognized for its harmonious blend of royal architecture, gardens, and agricultural traditions, so respectful, unhurried exploration is encouraged.
Many smaller shops and eateries close in the early afternoon, so plan meals and shopping around typical Spanish siesta timing.

places.name: Alcala de Henares
places.description: A UNESCO World Heritage university city and birthplace of Miguel de Cervantes, featuring a historic university, elegant plazas, and storks nesting on its rooftops. Its rich literary heritage and well-preserved old town make it a rewarding day trip.

activities.detail: Tour the Renaissance courtyards and the ornate Paraninfo hall of Cervantes' historic university, where Spain's top literary prize is awarded each year.
Visit the reconstructed birthplace of Miguel de Cervantes, now a free museum with period rooms and Don Quixote memorabilia.
Relax in the city's lively main square, ringed by cafes, gardens, and a statue of Cervantes himself.
See the only cathedral in the world with the title 'Magistral,' built where the child martyrs Justo and Pastor were venerated.
Step into one of Europe's oldest surviving public theaters, still hosting performances since the 1600s.
Look up across the old town's towers and belfries, where white storks build enormous nests each spring.
Admire the elliptical baroque church of the Bernardas convent, an architectural rarity in Spain.
Stroll beneath the medieval porticoed arcades of one of Europe's longest covered streets, lined with shops and cafes.

food.detail: Try Alcalá's famous candied almonds, roasted in caramelized sugar and sold in paper cones by local sweet shops.
Sample this layered puff-pastry cake filled with meat, vegetables, and béchamel, a traditional local specialty.
Buy handmade pastries and candied egg yolks sold through the turno (revolving window) of the Clarisas convent nuns.
Enjoy this Spanish take on French toast, soaked in wine or milk and fried, especially popular around Easter.

food.text: Menu del día in Plaza de Cervantes
food.detail: Sit at an outdoor terrace for an affordable multi-course lunch menu, a local midday ritual.

culture.text: Visit as an easy Madrid day trip
culture.detail: Cercanías trains (lines C2 and C7) connect Madrid to Alcalá de Henares in about 35-45 minutes.
Access to the Colegio de San Ildefonso's historic halls is often only possible via scheduled guided tours.
When buying pastries from cloistered nuns, transactions happen through a small revolving window with little direct conversation.
Some museums and shops close for a few hours in the early afternoon, so plan sightseeing around typical Spanish siesta hours.

places.name: Sierra de Guadarrama National Park
places.nameIt: Parque Nacional de la Sierra de Guadarrama
places.description: A mountain range national park offering pine forests, granite peaks, and alpine scenery just outside Madrid, popular for hiking and skiing. It provides a refreshing natural escape with panoramic views over the capital region.

activities.detail: Hike to the highest peak in the range at 2,428m for panoramic views over glacial cirques and the Madrid basin.
Wander among the park's famous granite rock formations, a paradise for climbers and boulderers.

activities.text: Ride the Puerto de Navacerrada pass
activities.detail: Visit this historic mountain pass, a hub for skiing in winter and hiking or mountain biking in summer.

activities.text: Walk the Valle de la Fuenfría
activities.detail: Follow a preserved stretch of Roman road through dense pine forest between Cercedilla and the Navacerrada pass.

activities.text: See the Circo de Peñalara lagoons
activities.detail: Discover glacial lakes like Laguna Grande de Peñalara formed during the last ice age.
Traverse the dramatic 'Seven Peaks' ridgeline with sweeping views across the sierra.
Board Madrid's scenic commuter rail line up into the mountains, a classic gateway into the park for day hikers.

food.detail: A hearty chickpea stew with meats and vegetables, popular in mountain villages after a day of hiking.
Large white beans stewed with chorizo and pork, a traditional dish from villages near the sierra.
Slow-roasted lamb cooked in a wood-fired oven, a staple of Castilian mountain cuisine.
Local trout from mountain streams, pan-fried with cured ham, often served in Cercedilla's restaurants.
A rich marzipan and custard cake worth seeking out on trips through nearby Segovia.

culture.detail: Certain zones of La Pedriza require advance registration to protect the fragile granite ecosystem.
Weather changes quickly at altitude, so pack warm clothing even in summer.
Madrileños flock to the park on weekends, so arrive early for parking and quieter trails.
The Cercedilla train and local buses reduce congestion and are a scenic way to reach trailheads.

places.name: Buitrago del Lozoya
places.description: A fortified medieval town encircled by well-preserved Moorish walls along the Lozoya River, notable for its castle and an unexpected Picasso museum. Its compact historic core makes for a charming and quick escape from the city.

activities.detail: Circle the well-preserved 12th-century mud-brick and masonry walls that almost completely encircle the old town.
A small but surprising museum holding original works, drawings and photos given by Picasso to his friend and barber, a native of Buitrago.
Climb around the ruined medieval castle of the Mendoza family perched at the edge of the walled enclosure above the river.

activities.text: Visit Iglesia de Santa María del Castillo
activities.detail: See this Mudéjar-style brick church built directly against the town walls, with its distinctive tower.
Walk over this medieval stone bridge spanning the Lozoya River just outside the old town.
Look for the unusual small bullring built directly into a section of the ancient town walls, still used during local festivals.
Relax in the town's central square lined with old houses, cafés and the town hall.

activities.text: Walk the riverside park along the Lozoya
activities.detail: Follow the shaded path beside the river for views back up at the walls and castle.

food.detail: Slow-roasted Castilian lamb, a signature dish of the wider Sierra Norte region around Buitrago.
Warming garlic soup made with bread, paprika and a poached egg, common in the cooler mountain towns near Madrid.
Freshwater trout from the nearby river, usually pan-fried with ham or almonds.
Fried breadcrumbs cooked with chorizo, garlic and paprika, a hearty shepherd's dish typical of the sierra.
Simple local ring-shaped pastries often enjoyed with coffee in the town's small bakeries and cafés.

culture.detail: The Picasso museum is free to enter and often overlooked, so it's usually quiet even when the town has visitors.
The entire walled old town can be explored on foot in under two hours, making it easy to combine with other Sierra Norte stops.
Being close to Madrid, the town gets busier with day-trippers on weekends, especially in spring and autumn.
Despite its museum-like walls, Buitrago is still inhabited, so keep noise down in residential lanes, especially during midday rest hours.

places.name: El Pardo
places.description: A royal palace and hunting estate on the outskirts of Madrid, historically used as a residence by Spanish monarchs and later Francisco Franco. Surrounded by protected woodland, it offers a quieter historical alternative to the busier city palaces.

activities.text: Tour the Palacio Real de El Pardo
activities.detail: Visit the royal palace where Spanish monarchs once resided and where Franco lived and governed for nearly four decades, seeing period furnishings and Goya-designed tapestries.

activities.text: See the Cristo Yacente at Capuchinos
activities.detail: Step into the Convento de Capuchinos to view Gregorio Fernández's renowned polychrome recumbent Christ sculpture, a masterpiece of Spanish Baroque religious art.

activities.text: Admire Goya's frescoes in the parish church
activities.detail: The Iglesia de San Antonio de El Pardo holds ceiling frescoes attributed to Francisco de Goya, a lesser-known work by the master.

activities.text: Walk or cycle through Monte de El Pardo
activities.detail: Explore Europe's largest holm oak forest near a capital city, a protected hunting estate turned nature reserve with deer and wild boar.
See this small neoclassical pavilion designed by architect Juan de Villanueva, built as a retreat for the future Charles IV.

activities.text: Photograph the palace gardens and courtyard
activities.detail: Wander the palace's formal gardens and inner courtyard, quieter and less crowded than central Madrid's royal sites.

food.detail: A hearty chickpea stew with meats and vegetables served in courses, a classic comfort dish found in local eateries around El Pardo village.
Slow-cooked tripe stew with chorizo and paprika, a traditional Madrid dish often featured on menus in the area's rustic restaurants.
Stewed venison or wild boar reflects El Pardo's history as a royal hunting estate, still served in nearby traditional restaurants.
A classic Spanish breakfast or snack of fried dough dipped in thick hot chocolate, easily found in cafés near the palace.
Simple ring-shaped pastries and baked goods sold in village bakeries, popular for a quick bite after touring the palace.

culture.detail: The palace occasionally closes for state functions since it still serves as an official government guesthouse.
As with many Spanish royal sites, photography inside the palace's furnished rooms is typically prohibited.
El Pardo feels more like a small town than a tourist hub, so services and crowds are much lighter than central Madrid.
Many visitors pair the palace visit with a walk in Monte de El Pardo, so wear comfortable shoes for uneven forest paths.

restaurants.name: Sobrino de Botín
restaurants.description: Recognized by Guinness World Records as the oldest restaurant in the world, famed for its wood-fired oven dating to 1725.

restaurants.name: Casa Lucio
restaurants.description: A beloved institution on Cava Baja famous for simple, perfectly executed home-style Madrid classics.

restaurants.name: DiverXO
restaurants.address: Calle Padre Damián 23 (NH Collection Eurobuilding), Madrid
restaurants.description: Chef Dabiz Muñoz's three-Michelin-star theatrical tasting menu blending Asian, French and Spanish influences.
restaurants.mustOrder: Tasting menu (changes seasonally, signature 'steamed bao' dishes)

restaurants.name: Coque
restaurants.description: The Sandoval family's multi-sensory dining experience moving guests through bar, cellar, kitchen and dining room.

restaurants.name: La Terraza del Casino
restaurants.description: Paco Roncero's elegant rooftop-adjacent dining room inside the historic Casino de Madrid offering refined tasting menus.

restaurants.name: Lhardy
restaurants.description: An 1839 institution with an ornate dining room, legendary for its slow-simmered stew served in silver tureens.

restaurants.name: Taberna La Bola
restaurants.description: A century-old family-run tavern cooking cocido over charcoal exactly as it has for generations.
restaurants.mustOrder: Cocido madrileño en puchero de barro

restaurants.name: Mercado de San Miguel
restaurants.address: Plaza de San Miguel s/n, Madrid
restaurants.description: An early 20th-century iron-and-glass market turned upscale food hall packed with tapas and pintxo stalls.
restaurants.mustOrder: Jamón ibérico and Spanish tortilla tapas

restaurants.name: StreetXO
restaurants.description: Dabiz Muñoz's boisterous street-food counter serving bold, punchy fusion bites in an energetic open kitchen setting.

restaurants.name: Santerra
restaurants.description: Chef Miguel Carretero's contemporary take on Spanish comfort food using top-tier seasonal produce.

restaurants.name: Casa José
restaurants.description: A former Michelin-starred house in the royal town of Aranjuez known for refined dishes built around local garden produce.

restaurants.name: Chirón
restaurants.description: A Michelin-starred family restaurant in Valdemoro praised for creative, technique-driven Spanish cuisine.

restaurants.name: Charolés
restaurants.address: Calle Floridablanca 24, San Lorenzo de El Escorial
restaurants.description: A well-known stop near the monastery renowned for its slow-cooked stew and classic roasts.

restaurants.name: Hostería del Estudiante
restaurants.address: Calle de los Colegios 3 (Parador de Alcalá), Alcalá de Henares
restaurants.description: Set in a historic university building, this Parador restaurant serves hearty regional classics in a storied setting.

restaurants.name: Mesón Cuevas del Vino
restaurants.description: A rustic mesón built into an old wine cellar in Chinchón's famous plaza, popular for wood-fired roasts.

regions/valencia.json.name: Valencian Community
regions/valencia.json.history: The Valencian Community has a rich history shaped by its location on Spain's eastern Mediterranean coast. The region was founded as a kingdom in the 13th century by James I of Aragon after the Reconquista, and it became a major center of trade and commerce during the medieval period. Valencia's golden age came during the 15th and 16th centuries when it rivaled Barcelona as a Mediterranean power, though it later declined under Spanish Habsburg rule. The region experienced significant development in the 18th and 19th centuries and today remains one of Spain's most economically dynamic areas.
regions/valencia.json.culturalNotes: The Valencian Community is famous for its vibrant festivals, most notably Las Fallas in March, where elaborate satirical floats are paraded and burned in celebration of spring. The region is deeply rooted in agricultural traditions, particularly rice cultivation in the Turia riverbed, which inspired the world-renowned paella dish. Valencian culture blends Mediterranean influences with a strong sense of regional identity, evident in its distinctive architecture, local cuisine, and the widespread use of the Valencian language in daily life.
regions/valencia.json.bestTimeToVisit: Spring for Las Fallas festival or fall for beach weather without summer crowds.
regions/valencia.json.languageNotes: The official language is Spanish, but Valencian is widely spoken and co-official in the region, representing a unique Romance language closely related to Catalan. Common Valencian phrases include "Bon dia" for good morning and "Adéu" for goodbye, and learning a few local words is appreciated by residents.

places.name: Valencia
places.description: The vibrant capital of the region, blending a historic old town with the futuristic City of Arts and Sciences. Its mix of Gothic architecture, world-class paella, and lively markets make it an essential stop.
imageQueries: Valencia City of Arts and Sciences

activities.detail: Explore Santiago Calatrava's futuristic complex of museums, an IMAX cinema, an oceanarium, and a science museum set along the old riverbed.
Climb the Gothic-Baroque cathedral's bell tower and see the chalice claimed by some to be the Holy Grail.
Wander this UNESCO-listed 15th-century silk exchange, a masterpiece of civil Gothic architecture with twisting columns.
Browse one of Europe's largest fresh food markets, housed in a stunning Art Nouveau iron-and-glass building.
Walk or cycle through the nine-kilometer park created from the diverted Turia riverbed, connecting much of the city.
Get lost in the narrow medieval streets of the old town, filled with street art, tapas bars, and centuries-old palaces.
Spend an afternoon on this wide sandy city beach lined with seafood restaurants and a lively promenade.
Take a boat ride on this freshwater lagoon south of the city, the birthplace of paella and a haven for birdlife.

food.detail: Try the original version of Spain's famous rice dish, made with rabbit, chicken, and flat green beans rather than seafood.
Sip the sweet, milky tiger-nut drink alongside soft, sugar-glazed pastries at a traditional horchatería.
Sample the local brunch cocktail mixing cava, orange juice, vodka, and gin, often served in a shared pitcher.
Taste this rustic eel stew from the Albufera region, cooked with potatoes, garlic, and paprika.
Order this simple tapa of shredded salt cod and roasted red peppers dressed in olive oil.
Try this noodle-based cousin of paella, typically made with seafood and a rich sofrito base.

culture.detail: Most restaurants don't open for lunch until 2pm and dinner rarely starts before 9pm, so plan meal times accordingly.
If visiting in mid-March, expect fireworks, street parties, and giant satirical effigies burned during this iconic UNESCO-listed festival.
Small shops often close for a few hours in the early afternoon, especially outside the main tourist areas.
Locals traditionally eat paella at midday, often on Sundays, rather than for dinner.

places.name: Alicante
places.description: A sunny coastal city crowned by the imposing Santa Bárbara Castle overlooking the Mediterranean. Its palm-lined Explanada promenade and lively beaches make it a favorite gateway to the Costa Blanca.

activities.detail: Climb or take the free elevator from Postiguet beach up to this hilltop fortress for sweeping views over the city and the Mediterranean.
Walk the famous wave-patterned marble promenade lined with palm trees, street performers, and seaside cafes.
This central sandy beach sits right beneath the castle and is perfect for a quick swim or sunbathing session.
Get lost in the old quarter's narrow, colorful, flower-pot-lined streets climbing up toward the castle.
Explore award-winning exhibits on Iberian, Roman, and medieval history of the region.
Take a short ferry from the harbor to Spain's smallest inhabited island, known for clear waters and a marine reserve.
Sample fresh seafood, local produce, and jamón at this bustling early 20th-century market hall.

activities.text: See the Basilica of Santa María
activities.detail: Step into Alicante's oldest church, built in Gothic style atop a former mosque.

food.detail: A local rice dish cooked in fish and seafood stock, typically served with allioli on the side.
A hard almond-and-honey nougat that originates from the wider Alicante province, especially Xixona.
A hearty rice dish with rabbit and snails, a staple of inland Alicante cuisine.
Try cured, salted fish like mojama or dried tuna roe, a Mediterranean tradition sold in local markets.
A flatbread topped with tuna, tomato, and peppers, popular as a light snack or tapa.
Pair the cold tiger-nut drink with sweet glazed pastries at a local horchatería.

culture.detail: Lunch is typically eaten around 2-3pm and dinner rarely starts before 9pm, so plan accordingly.
In late June the city holds massive bonfire and fireworks celebrations similar to Valencia's Las Fallas, so expect crowds and noise.

culture.text: Both Spanish and Valencian are spoken
culture.detail: Street signs and announcements often appear in both languages, reflecting the region's bilingual identity.
It's considered impolite to walk around town in swimsuits away from the immediate beachfront area.

places.name: Peñíscola
places.description: A picturesque walled town on a rocky peninsula, topped by the dramatic Papa Luna Castle. Its narrow whitewashed streets and golden beaches have made it a beloved filming location and seaside escape.

activities.detail: Climb the 13th-century castle built by the Knights Templar and later home to Pope Benedict XIII (Papa Luna), with sweeping sea views from its ramparts.
Get lost in the whitewashed, flower-lined alleys of the walled old quarter, many of which appeared in the TV series Game of Thrones.

activities.text: Walk the city walls and Bufador
activities.detail: Follow the ancient fortified walls to the Bufador, a natural blowhole in the rocks where waves crash dramatically during rough seas.
Spend an afternoon on this long sandy beach with calm, shallow waters and full views of the castle and old town skyline.

activities.text: Visit the Ermita de la Virgen de la Ermitana
activities.detail: Hike or drive up to this hilltop hermitage just outside town for panoramic coastal views and a peaceful pilgrimage site.
Enjoy a sunset walk along the seafront promenade lined with palm trees, restaurants, and views back toward the illuminated castle.

activities.text: Boat trip along the Costa del Azahar
activities.detail: Take a short boat excursion to see the peninsula and castle from the water, especially striking at golden hour.

food.detail: A classic Valencian coastal rice dish cooked in fish stock and served with the seafood on the side, a specialty of Peñíscola's fishing tradition.
A hearty fisherman's stew of local catch, potatoes, and saffron broth, reflecting the town's deep ties to the sea.
Sweet, prized red prawns from the nearby waters, often served simply grilled or boiled to highlight their natural flavor.
The region's iconic rice dish with chicken, rabbit, and beans, widely available and best enjoyed at a seafront terrace.
A noodle-based cousin of paella made with short pasta and seafood, popular along this stretch of coast.
A refreshing tiger-nut drink paired with soft sugary pastries, a beloved Valencian sweet treat for hot afternoons.

culture.text: Respect the old town's residential character
culture.detail: Many houses within the walls are still lived in, so keep noise down in the evenings as you wander the narrow lanes.

culture.text: Visit the castle early or late
culture.detail: Arrive near opening time or a couple hours before closing to avoid tour groups and midday heat on the exposed ramparts.

culture.text: Time your visit around local fiestas
culture.detail: The Fiestas Patronales in September honor the Virgen de la Ermitana with processions, fireworks, and traditional bull-running events.

culture.text: Parking is limited near the old town
culture.detail: Use the designated lots outside the walls, since vehicle access to the historic center is restricted for most visitors.

places.name: Xàtiva
places.description: A historic town dominated by its twin castle perched on a ridge above the rooftops. Rich in Roman, Moorish, and Christian heritage, it offers stunning views and a charming old quarter.

activities.detail: Explore the twin castle complex, Castell Menor and Castell Major, linked by a walled ridge with sweeping views over the Costera plain.
Visit this Renaissance former grain exchange housing archaeological finds and the famous inverted portrait of Felipe V.
See the grand collegiate church, known locally as La Seu, with its Gothic and Baroque elements in the old quarter.
Stroll the old Jewish and Moorish quarter's narrow lanes beneath the castle for a sense of the town's layered past.
Stop by this iconic lion-fountain landmark that has been a meeting point in the town center for centuries.
Admire the Gothic facade of the Royal Hospital, one of the town's most striking medieval buildings.
Walk along the old bridges and irrigation channels of the Albuera area for a quieter, greener side of Xàtiva.
Head just outside town to this significant prehistoric archaeological cave site set in a scenic gorge.

food.detail: A hearty winter rice dish cooked with beans and turnips, a signature comfort food of the Costera region.
A traditional stew of local vegetables, pork, and rice simmered together in a clay pot.
A flatbread topped with tuna, tomato, and onion, popular as a snack or light lunch in local bakeries.
Sample local pastry shops for traditional almond and honey sweets tied to Valencian confectionery traditions.
Cool off with the classic Valencian tigernut drink paired with sweet elongated pastries, found in local cafés.

food.text: Local Costera olive oil and wine
food.detail: Try regional extra virgin olive oil and DO Valencia wines often featured in Xàtiva's traditional restaurants.

culture.text: Visit early or late for the castle
culture.detail: Arrive in the morning or late afternoon to avoid midday heat while climbing the steep path to the castle.
If visiting in August, expect the town's major annual fair with processions, markets, and local festivities.
The old quarter and castle paths involve cobblestones and steep inclines, so comfortable footwear is essential.
Many small shops and some sights close for a few hours in the early afternoon, typical of Spanish small-town rhythms.

places.name: Calpe
places.description: A lively seaside town dominated by the massive limestone Peñón de Ifach rock jutting into the sea. It offers beautiful beaches, a fishing harbor, and great hiking up the iconic rock.

activities.detail: Follow the trail and tunnel carved through the rock up to the summit of this 332-metre limestone outcrop for sweeping views over the Costa Blanca.
This long sandy beach lined with cafés and palm trees is the town's main stretch of shoreline, right beneath the Peñón.
Walk around this pink-tinged salt lagoon, a protected wetland where flamingos and other wading birds gather.
Stroll the old town's narrow whitewashed streets, medieval walls, and colourful street art near the Iglesia Antigua.

activities.text: Visit the fishing port and lonja
activities.detail: See the working harbour where the local fleet lands its catch and, in the late afternoon, watch the fast-paced fish auction.

activities.text: See the Baños de la Reina
activities.detail: Explore these Roman-era fish-salting pools and ruins cut into the rocks near the seafront.

food.detail: A classic Alicante-style rice cooked in fish stock and served separately from the seafood it's flavoured with, a Calpe fishing-town staple.
A local noodle version of paella, cooked with seafood and often a garlicky alioli on the side.
A hearty fishermen's stew of fresh local fish, potatoes, and a rich saffron-tinted broth.
A cold roasted vegetable salad of peppers, aubergine, and salt cod, typical of the Valencian coast.
Sample whatever fish or seafood was auctioned that day at the port, served simply grilled in the harbourside restaurants.

culture.detail: Start the ascent in the morning to avoid midday heat and crowds, and stick to the marked path as the rock is a protected natural park.
The lonja near the port holds a lively fish auction in the late afternoon that's worth watching even if you're not buying.
Many small shops and some restaurants close in the early afternoon before reopening in the evening, especially outside peak summer season.
Calpe's Moros y Cristianos festival in late October fills the streets with parades, costumes, and mock battles celebrating the town's history.

places.name: Dénia
places.description: A charming port town overlooked by its hilltop castle, known as the gateway to Ibiza and Formentera ferries. It boasts great beaches, a bustling marina, and renowned rice cuisine as Spain's Gastronomy Region capital.

activities.detail: Explore this hilltop fortress with Moorish and medieval layers offering sweeping views over the town, port, and Montgó mountain.
Wander the lively harbor where ferries depart for Ibiza and Formentera and fishing boats unload their daily catch.
Swim in the clear, rocky coves of this protected marine reserve just south of town, ideal for snorkeling among posidonia meadows.
Enjoy the long sandy stretch north of the port, backed by chiringuitos and calm shallow waters.
Trek the trails of this dramatic limestone massif that dominates Dénia's skyline, rewarding climbers with coastal panoramas.
Browse this bustling covered market for fresh red prawns, local produce, and a genuine taste of Denia daily life.
Get lost in the whitewashed lanes below the castle, dotted with tapas bars, boutiques, and historic churches.

food.detail: A rice dish cooked in fish stock and served separately from the seafood, a Dénia classic born from local fishermen's traditions.
The prized red shrimp caught off Dénia's coast, prized for its sweet flavor and often eaten simply boiled or grilled.
A noodle-based cousin of paella that originated in this region, typically made with seafood and a garlicky alioli on the side.
A rustic stew of eel, potatoes, garlic, and paprika, reflecting the Valencian coast's rich fishing heritage.
A cold roasted vegetable dish of peppers, eggplant, and onion dressed in olive oil, a refreshing local starter.

culture.detail: Since Dénia is the main port for Ibiza and Formentera, ferry tickets sell out fast in summer, so reserve ahead.
Restaurants often don't open for dinner until 8:30 or 9pm, following typical Spanish meal times.
Dénia holds UNESCO Creative City of Gastronomy status, so expect a strong local pride in rice dishes and seafood quality.

culture.text: Dress modestly for the castle and churches
culture.detail: As with many historic Spanish sites, cover shoulders and knees when visiting religious or heritage buildings.

places.name: Benidorm
places.description: A high-rise beach resort city famous for its skyscraper skyline and long golden beaches. It's the entertainment and nightlife capital of the Costa Blanca, packed with theme parks and lively promenades.

activities.detail: Stroll or sunbathe on this bustling golden-sand beach backed by Benidorm's iconic skyscraper skyline.
Take in panoramic sea views from this clifftop viewpoint in the old town separating Levante and Poniente beaches.
Ride roller coasters through ancient-civilization themed zones at one of Spain's biggest amusement parks.
Take a glass-bottom boat to this small rocky island for snorkeling and views back at the coastline.
Cool off on slides and wave pools at one of Europe's most popular water parks.
Wander the whitewashed old town's narrow lanes, small squares, and the Sant Jaume church perched above the sea.
Bar-hop along the British-style pub zone and cabaret venues that make Benidorm the Costa Blanca's party capital.

food.detail: A Valencian-style rice dish cooked in fish stock and served separately from the seafood it's flavored with.
A noodle-based cousin of paella, typically made with shrimp, squid, and other seafood.
Flat savory pastries topped with vegetables, tuna, or sardines, popular as a quick snack.
A sweet tiger-nut drink paired with soft sugary pastries, best enjoyed at a terrace café.
Reflecting the huge British expat and tourist presence, many strip cafés serve classic fry-ups all day.

food.text: Tapas crawl in the old town
food.detail: Sample small plates like patatas bravas and jamón at traditional bars tucked into the historic quarter.

culture.detail: Due to massive British and northern European tourism, menus and signage are often in English alongside Spanish.
Restaurants fill up from around 9pm onward, following typical Spanish dining hours.
Colored flags indicate swimming safety conditions and are strictly enforced by lifeguards.
Benidorm is geared toward lively mass tourism, so expect a party atmosphere rather than a sleepy Spanish village vibe.

places.name: Xàbia
places.description: An elegant coastal town with dramatic cliffs, the iconic Granadella cove, and a charming old quarter. It combines upscale beach life with stunning natural scenery along the Costa Blanca.

activities.detail: Swim in the turquoise waters of this pine-backed cove often ranked among Spain's most beautiful beaches, best visited early morning to beat the crowds.

activities.text: Cap de Sant Antoni lighthouse walk
activities.detail: Walk or cycle the cliffside path to this lighthouse and nature reserve for sweeping views over the Mediterranean and Montgó massif.
Wander narrow stone streets around the fortified Gothic church of Sant Bartomeu, built partly for defense against pirate raids.
Climb the distinctive Montgó massif for panoramic views stretching from Xàbia to Dénia and the open sea.
Stroll the harbor with its fishing boats and continue to the Arenal beach strip lined with cafes and palm trees.
Kayak or hike to this dramatic hand-carved coastal cave accessible only by sea or a rugged cliffside trail.

activities.text: Museu Arqueològic i Etnogràfic Soler Blasco
activities.detail: Learn about the town's Iberian, Roman, and maritime history in this small museum housed in a historic building.

food.detail: A classic Valencian coastal rice dish cooked in fish stock, typically served with the seafood on the side.
A noodle-based cousin of paella made with short pasta and local seafood, often finished with alioli.
A cold roasted vegetable dish of peppers, eggplant, and salt cod, dressed simply with olive oil.
A traditional fisherman's stew of boiled local fish and potatoes, usually served with alioli on the side.
Xàbia's hillsides are historically known for sun-dried raisins and sweet moscatell wine, a legacy of its agricultural past.
Sample just-landed prawns, red mullet, and cuttlefish at the small restaurants near the fishing harbor.

culture.detail: Xàbia has a historic hilltop old town and a separate beachfront port area, so orient yourself before planning your day.
Parking at Cala Granadella is restricted in summer, so arrive very early or use the shuttle bus service.
Both Valencian (Catalan) and Spanish are used locally, with Xàbia and Jávea both being accepted names for the town.
Xàbia attracts a more relaxed, affluent crowd than nearby party towns, so nightlife is low-key and dining leans toward long, leisurely meals.

restaurants.name: Casa Montaña
restaurants.description: Legendary standing-room-only tapas bar in a historic building serving traditional Spanish bites with an extensive wine list.
restaurants.mustOrder: Boquerones en vinagre with local wines

restaurants.name: Horchatería Santa Catalina
restaurants.description: Historic horchata and fartons shop dating to 1836, perfect for a sweet Valencian experience.

restaurants.name: Restaurante Navarro
restaurants.description: Contemporary restaurant blending traditional Valencian ingredients with innovative techniques in an intimate setting.
restaurants.mustOrder: All i pebre (traditional eel and potato stew)

restaurants.name: Casa Montaña Vertical
restaurants.description: Sister establishment to Casa Montaña offering elevated tapas in a more comfortable seated environment.
restaurants.mustOrder: Jamón ibérico with pan con tomate

restaurants.name: El Pepica
restaurants.description: Beachfront paella restaurant with direct sea views, serving traditional rice dishes and fresh catch daily.

restaurants.name: Casa Montaña
restaurants.description: Family-run establishment in the historic heart of Xàtiva serving regional specialties with local charm.
restaurants.mustOrder: Espinacas con pasas y piñones (spinach with raisins and pine nuts)

restaurants.name: Vertical
restaurants.description: Avant-garde fine dining restaurant offering a creative tasting menu in a sleek, minimalist setting.
restaurants.mustOrder: Chef's tasting menu with wine pairings

restaurants.name: Palmira
restaurants.description: Beachfront classic known for authentic paellas and fresh seafood prepared simply and well.
restaurants.mustOrder: Arroz a banda (rice cooked in fish stock)

restaurants.name: Los Pescaítos
restaurants.description: Contemporary seafood restaurant celebrating the Mediterranean catch with elegant preparations.

restaurants.name: Casa Pacheco
restaurants.description: Historic restaurant in wine country serving hearty regional dishes paired with local Requena wines.
restaurants.mustOrder: Rabo de toro (oxtail stew) with Requena wine

restaurants.name: La Pepica
restaurants.description: Right on the beach beneath the historic castle, serving contemporary Mediterranean seafood with dramatic sea views.

restaurants.name: Ricard Camarena
restaurants.description: Michelin-starred restaurant pushing culinary boundaries with technical innovation and Valencian roots.

restaurants.name: Casa Montaña
restaurants.description: Mountain town establishment serving authentic regional cuisine in a warm, traditional setting.

restaurants.name: El Tollo
restaurants.description: Beach restaurant famous for paella and fresh catch, serving the Valencia coast for generations.

restaurants.name: Oca Bruja
restaurants.description: Contemporary restaurant celebrating seasonal vegetables and local ingredients with artistic presentation.

regions/basque_country.json.name: Basque Country
regions/basque_country.json.history: The Basque Country has one of Europe's most distinct and ancient cultures, with evidence of human settlement dating back millennia. During the Middle Ages, the region developed its own fueros, or charters, granting it considerable autonomy within the Spanish kingdoms. The industrial revolution transformed the Basque territories, particularly around Bilbao, making them an economic powerhouse. The Spanish Civil War and Franco's dictatorship brought severe repression to Basque language and culture, though the region has since recovered its autonomy and celebrated heritage.
regions/basque_country.json.culturalNotes: The Basque people maintain a fiercely independent identity expressed through their unique language, distinctive cuisine, and strong regional pride. Traditional txoko social clubs, pelota ball games, and bertsolari poetry competitions remain central to Basque social life and cultural expression. The region is known for its culinary excellence, with pintxos bar snacks and innovative gastronomy drawing visitors worldwide.
regions/basque_country.json.bestTimeToVisit: Summer months offer the best weather for exploring the coast and cities.
regions/basque_country.json.languageNotes: Euskera, the Basque language, is a linguistic isolate unrelated to Spanish or any other European language, and has experienced a major revival since the end of Franco's regime. Common greetings include "Kaixo" (hello) and "Eskerrik asko" (thank you very much), and many locals appreciate visitors who make an effort to use Basque words.

places.name: Bilbao
places.description: The largest city in the Basque Country, transformed from an industrial port into a vibrant cultural hub anchored by the Guggenheim Museum. Its atmospheric old town, riverside promenades, and superb pintxo bars make it an essential stop.

activities.detail: Frank Gehry's titanium-clad masterpiece houses contemporary art and is itself the city's iconic landmark, complete with Jeff Koons' giant flower sculpture Puppy outside.
Explore the medieval Siete Calles (Seven Streets) packed with shops, churches, and pintxo bars around Plaza Nueva.
Take the century-old transporter bridge, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, across the Nervión estuary between Portugalete and Getxo.
Walk or cycle along the revitalized riverfront past modern bridges like Zubizuri and public art installations.

activities.text: Visit the Museo de Bellas Artes
activities.detail: See an outstanding collection of Basque, Spanish, and European art spanning from medieval times to the 20th century.
Ride the funicular up Mount Artxanda for panoramic views over the city and surrounding green hills.
Browse one of Europe's largest covered markets, a great spot for fresh produce, seafood, and casual pintxos.

food.detail: Hop between bars in Casco Viejo and Plaza Nueva sampling bite-sized creations like gilda (anchovy, olive, pepper) and txalupa (stuffed pastry boats).
Salt cod slow-cooked in olive oil and garlic, emulsified into a signature Basque sauce.
Thick-cut, bone-in beef chop grilled over coals, a beloved staple of Basque cuisine served rare and smoky.
A hearty tuna and potato stew traditionally made by Basque fishermen, warming and rustic.
Pair the slightly sparkling, crisp local white wine with fresh anchovies or grilled fish.
Finish a meal with creamy arroz con leche or a wedge of smoky sheep's milk Idiazabal cheese, often with quince paste.

culture.detail: Pintxos are typically eaten standing at the bar with a small drink, and you keep track of your toothpicks to tally the bill.
Euskara (Basque) is co-official with Spanish, so many signs and menus appear in both languages.
Many shops close for a few hours in early afternoon, and locals eat dinner quite late, often after 9pm.
Basque culture and identity are strong and distinct from the rest of Spain, so showing interest in local customs and language is appreciated.

places.name: San Sebastián
places.description: An elegant seaside city famed for its crescent-shaped La Concha beach and one of the highest concentrations of Michelin-starred restaurants in the world. Its old quarter is packed with legendary pintxo bars, making it a food lover's paradise.

activities.detail: Stroll the curving promenade along one of Europe's most beautiful urban beaches, framed by Monte Igueldo and Monte Urgull.
Climb the wooded hill above the old town to the Castillo de la Mota and the Sagrado Corazón statue for panoramic bay views.
Take the historic funicular up Monte Igueldo for sweeping views and an old-fashioned amusement park at the summit.
Wander the narrow lanes packed with pintxo bars, tiny plazas, and the Baroque Basilica de Santa María del Coro.
Discover Basque history, art, and ethnography in a renovated 16th-century convent at the foot of Monte Urgull.

activities.text: See the Peine del Viento sculptures
activities.detail: Admire Chillida's iconic iron sculptures embedded in the rocks at the western end of Ondarreta beach.
Visit this nearby fishing village, birthplace of Juan Sebastián Elcano, known for grilled fish and Getariako Txakolina wine.

food.detail: Hop between bars in the old town sampling bite-sized creations like gilda (anchovy, olive, guindilla pepper) and tortilla de patata.
Try this classic Basque salt cod dish emulsified in olive oil and garlic into a silky, garlicky sauce.
Seek out a thick, well-aged rib steak cooked over coals, a Basque grill-house specialty.
Sample this smoky sheep's milk cheese from the Basque highlands, often paired with quince paste.
Drink the slightly sparkling, crisp local white wine, traditionally poured from a height to aerate it.
Splurge on a multi-course meal at one of the city's renowned fine-dining restaurants showcasing modern Basque cuisine.

culture.detail: Order drinks and pintxos at the bar, keep your toothpicks and napkins as they're often used to tally your bill.
Euskara (Basque) is widely spoken and seen on signs alongside Spanish, reflecting strong regional identity.
Locals typically eat dinner from 9pm onward, so restaurants and bars stay lively late into the evening.
July and August bring large crowds to La Concha, so arrive early to claim a good spot on the sand.

places.name: Hondarribia
places.description: A charming fishing town on the French border known for its colorful balconied houses and well-preserved fortified old quarter. Its picturesque marina and proximity to the Bidasoa river make it a delightful coastal stop.

activities.detail: Stroll the cobbled old quarter with its coats of arms, stone walls, and centuries-old fortified houses.

activities.text: See the colorful balconies of Calle Mayor and Calle San Pedro
activities.detail: Admire the wooden balconies painted in deep reds and greens, a signature feature of Basque fishing town architecture.

activities.text: Visit the Castle of Charles V
activities.detail: This 10th-century fortress turned Parador hotel dominates the old town and once defended the border with France.

activities.text: Explore the marina and fishing port
activities.detail: Watch working fishing boats unload their catch in a lively harbor still central to local life.

activities.text: Walk or cycle to Cabo Higuer lighthouse
activities.detail: A scenic coastal path leads to this lighthouse with sweeping views over the Bay of Biscay and French coast.

activities.text: Take the small ferry across the Bidasoa to Hendaye
activities.detail: A quick boat crossing lets you hop into France for lunch or a look at another side of the border region.
A sheltered sandy beach near the marina, popular with families for calm swimming waters.

activities.text: Visit the Ermita de Nuestra Señora de Guadalupe
activities.detail: This hilltop hermitage offers panoramic views and is the focus of a traditional pilgrimage each September.

food.detail: Spider crab meat mixed with tomato and brandy, often served baked in its own shell.

food.text: Pintxos crawl in the old town
food.detail: Hop between small bars along Calle San Pedro for bite-sized Basque tapas topped with seafood or cured meats.

food.text: Bacalao al pil-pil or a la vizcaína
food.detail: Salted cod cooked in olive oil emulsion or a rich red pepper sauce, a Basque coastal staple.
A warm corn flatbread folded around grilled Basque chorizo sausage, a popular street snack.
A slightly sparkling, crisp white wine from the Basque coast, traditionally poured from a height to aerate it.
Grilled or marinated straight from the local fishing fleet, best enjoyed at a portside restaurant.

culture.detail: Each September 8th the town holds a historic military procession commemorating a 1638 siege victory, drawing huge crowds.

culture.text: Basque and Spanish are both used
culture.detail: Street signs and menus often appear in Euskara as well as Spanish, reflecting strong local Basque identity.
Lunch is typically served from 1:30-3:30pm and dinner rarely starts before 8:30pm, as is standard in Spain.

culture.text: The border crossing to France is casual and quick
culture.detail: Locals frequently cross via bridge or ferry to Hendaye for shopping or dining, so bring ID just in case.

places.name: Zumaia
places.description: A coastal town renowned for the dramatic Flysch rock formations, layered cliffs revealing 60 million years of geological history. It's a favorite for scenic coastal walks and was a filming location for Game of Thrones.

activities.detail: Walk along Itzurun Beach to see the dramatic layered flysch rock formations that record 60 million years of geological history.
Visit this small geology center to understand the formation of the flysch and the Basque Coast Geopark before heading to the cliffs.

activities.text: Boat trip along the Flysch coast
activities.detail: Take a boat excursion from Zumaia or nearby Getaria to view the towering flysch cliffs from the sea for a full perspective.
Climb to this small chapel perched on a headland overlooking the sea, a scenic spot also used in filming locations.
Recognize Itzurun Beach as the filming location for Dragonstone scenes in Game of Thrones Season 7.
Hike a section of the Basque Coast trail offering sweeping views of cliffs, sea, and countryside between Zumaia and Deba.
Wander the historic center with its narrow streets, the Church of San Pedro, and views over the Urola River estuary.
Visit the former home and studio of the famous Basque painter, located just outside town, showcasing his works and art collection.

food.detail: A hearty Basque tuna and potato stew traditionally made by fishermen, perfect for the coastal climate.
Try this slightly sparkling, crisp local white wine, often poured from a height to aerate it before serving with pintxos.
Salted cod cooked slowly in olive oil and garlic to create a naturally emulsified sauce, a Basque culinary classic.
Squid cooked in their own ink, served as a rich, dark sauce often accompanied by rice.
Sample small Basque tapas at local bars, ranging from anchovies to txistorra sausage skewers.
Finish a meal with this traditional Basque layered dessert of cream, sponge cake, and caramel.

culture.text: Check tide times before visiting the flysch
culture.detail: The best views and safest walking along Itzurun Beach depend on low tide, so plan your visit accordingly.
Expect signs in Euskara (Basque) alongside Spanish, reflecting the strong regional identity of the area.
Many small shops and restaurants close for a few hours in the early afternoon, typical of Basque coastal towns.
Since Itzurun Beach draws Game of Thrones fans, be respectful of other visitors and any restricted areas near the cliffs.

places.name: San Juan de Gaztelugatxe
places.description: A tiny islet crowned by a hermitage, connected to the mainland by a dramatic zigzagging stone bridge, made globally famous as a filming location for Game of Thrones. Climbing its 200-plus steps rewards visitors with stunning Cantabrian Sea views.

activities.text: Climb the stone stairway to the hermitage
activities.detail: Ascend the famous zigzagging bridge and the more than 200 stone steps up to the small hermitage of San Juan Bautista perched on the islet's summit.

activities.text: Ring the chapel bell three times
activities.detail: Follow the old local custom of ringing the bell inside the hermitage three times and making a wish, said to ward off bad luck.

activities.text: Photograph the zigzag bridge from the viewpoint
activities.detail: Head to the Mirador de San Juan de Gaztelugatxe on the approach path for the classic postcard view of the winding stone bridge snaking out to the islet.

activities.text: Walk the coastal path from Bakio
activities.detail: Hike the scenic cliff-top trail connecting Bakio to Gaztelugatxe for dramatic Cantabrian coastline views before reaching the islet.

activities.text: Spot Game of Thrones filming locations
activities.detail: Recognize the islet and surrounding coastline as Dragonstone from Game of Thrones, a pilgrimage spot for fans of the series.

activities.text: Visit nearby San Pelaio and the small port
activities.detail: Explore the quiet hamlet of San Pelaio just above the site, with a small harbor and additional coastal viewpoints.

food.detail: Sample this classic Basque salt-cod dish emulsified in olive oil and garlic, found in restaurants along the nearby coast.
Try the crisp, slightly sparkling local white wine typical of the Bizkaia coast, often poured with a dramatic high splash.
Enjoy anchovies fresh from the Cantabrian Sea, simply grilled or marinated in vinegar as boquerones.
Warm up with this hearty Basque fisherman's stew of tuna, potatoes, and peppers, popular in coastal villages nearby.
Head to the fishing town of Bermeo a short drive away for a pintxo crawl featuring seafood-topped bites and local cider.

culture.detail: The site and its limited parking get extremely busy midday, especially in summer, so early morning visits are recommended.
Access to the islet requires a reserved time slot booked in advance, especially during peak tourist season.
The steep stone steps and uneven bridge surface can be slippery, so proper footwear is advised over sandals.
The chapel is still an active place of worship, so visitors should behave respectfully and keep noise to a minimum inside.

places.name: Oñati
places.description: A historic town in the Basque interior known for its striking Renaissance university building and as the gateway to the sacred Arantzazu sanctuary and Aizkorri mountains. It offers a glimpse of traditional Basque architecture away from the coast.

activities.detail: Wander the arcaded Renaissance courtyard of this 16th-century university building, the first in the Basque Country and one of Spain's finest civil Plateresque monuments.
Admire the baroque facade of the Ayuntamiento with its balconies and coats of arms, then linger at a terrace on this arcaded main square.
Visit this striking modernist mountain sanctuary with a spiky facade by Sáenz de Oiza, doors by Chillida, and frescoes by Oteiza, perched dramatically above a gorge.
Set off from Arantzazu toward Aizkorri, the highest peak in Gipuzkoa, through beech forests and limestone karst scenery sacred to Basque shepherding culture.
Cross the small stone bridges over the Deba's tributary streams while strolling Kale Zaharra and Bidebarrieta, lined with noble houses and coats of arms.

activities.text: San Miguel Church and hanging bridge
activities.detail: See the church built partly over the river, with a curious enclosed bridge-passage crossing the water beside it.
If visiting in season, look for traces of Oñati's unique carnival characters like the Zaldiko and Ziripot, among the most distinctive in the Basque Country.

activities.text: Day trip to Idiazabal cheese country
activities.detail: Explore the surrounding hills where shepherds still produce smoked Idiazabal cheese, a protected Basque specialty.

food.detail: Sample this smoky sheep's-milk cheese from local flocks, often served with walnuts and quince paste.
Try the town's hearty red bean stew, traditionally cooked with chorizo, pork, and cabbage.
Eat a warm corn-flour flatbread filled with grilled txistorra sausage, a favorite snack at local festivals.
Visit a nearby sagardotegi to drink natural cider poured from height, paired with cod omelette and grilled steak.
Finish a meal with this layered Basque sponge, cream, and caramel dessert popular in Gipuzkoa's inland towns.

culture.detail: Euskera is widely spoken day-to-day, so greetings like 'Kaixo' and 'Eskerrik asko' are appreciated.
Many small shops and some restaurants close on Sunday afternoons and Mondays, especially outside peak season.
The sanctuary is an active place of worship, so dress modestly and keep voices low inside the basilica.
Carnival and the Corpus Christi 'Corpus' processions with flower carpets are major local events worth planning a visit around.

places.name: Laguardia
places.description: A fortified medieval hilltop town in Basque wine country (Rioja Alavesa), surrounded by vineyards and known for wineries built into its ancient tunnels. Its walled old town and panoramic vineyard views make it a top day trip from Vitoria-Gasteiz.

activities.detail: Stroll the well-preserved ramparts and stone gates that still encircle the old town, offering sweeping views over the vineyards of Rioja Alavesa.

activities.text: Iglesia de Santa María de los Reyes
activities.detail: Marvel at the extraordinary 14th-century polychrome Gothic portico, one of the best-preserved painted church facades in Spain.
Tour the centuries-old cuevas carved beneath the town, many still used as working bodegas for aging Rioja wine.
An interactive museum near the old town explaining Rioja Alavesa winemaking, with tastings and a short multimedia film.
Admire Santiago Calatrava's undulating aluminum-and-wood winery set dramatically against the Sierra de Cantabria, just outside town.

activities.text: Climb San Juan Bautista bell tower
activities.detail: Climb this church tower for one of the best panoramic views of the walled town and surrounding vineyard patchwork.
Look for the carved coats of arms above house doorways along the main street, remnants of noble medieval families.

activities.text: Day trip to a hillside vineyard viewpoint
activities.detail: Just outside Laguardia, pull over at a roadside mirador for classic postcard views of vines rolling toward the mountains.

food.detail: Lamb chops grilled over burning vine cuttings, a signature Rioja Alavesa preparation often served at wine harvest gatherings.
A hearty potato and chorizo stew simmered with local paprika, a classic regional comfort dish.
Sample robust reds directly at a bodega built into the town's ancient tunnels, often paired with a cellar tour.
Snails cooked in a spicy tomato and chorizo sauce, a traditional tapa found in local taverns.
Hop between small bars for bite-sized pintxos topped with local cheese, cured meats, or piquillo peppers.
A firm sheep's milk cheese from the region, often served as a simple starter alongside a glass of crianza.

culture.detail: Cars are largely restricted inside the compact walled town, so use the designated lots just beyond the old gates.
Many underground wine cellars and wineries require reservations, especially for tours in English or during harvest season.

culture.text: Visit during the grape harvest festival
culture.detail: Late September's Fiestas de la Virgen de los Remedios and the wine harvest celebrations bring processions, tastings, and traditional dance.
Many shops and small bodegas close for a couple of hours around lunchtime, following typical Spanish siesta hours.

restaurants.name: Akelarre
restaurants.description: Three-Michelin-star restaurant with innovative takes on Basque tradition and breathtaking Bay of Biscay views.
restaurants.mustOrder: Kokoतsu (Sea urchin with kelp and uni)

restaurants.name: Martín Berasategui
restaurants.description: Three-Michelin-star temple of haute cuisine near San Sebastián with exquisite tasting menus and impeccable service.

restaurants.name: Arzak
restaurants.address: Avenida Alcalde José Elosegui, 273, San Sebastián
restaurants.description: Three-Michelin-star iconic restaurant where creativity meets tradition in dishes that challenge and delight.
restaurants.mustOrder: Txuleta de bonito (Bonito chop with Basque technique)

restaurants.name: Asador Etxebarri
restaurants.description: One-Michelin-star temple to grilled meats and fish cooked over open flames in a traditional txoko setting.

restaurants.name: Víctor Montes
restaurants.description: Legendary cooking club atmosphere with excellent pintxos and hearty Basque fare in historic Plaza Nueva.

restaurants.name: Guria
restaurants.description: Family-run classic in the old town serving generous portions of authentic Basque cuisine since 1945.
restaurants.mustOrder: Kokotxas de bacalao (Cod cheeks in pil-pil sauce)

restaurants.name: El Perro Chico
restaurants.description: Cozy pintxos bar and wine shop where locals gather for creative small bites and exceptional Basque wines.

restaurants.name: Tolona
restaurants.description: Modern Basque kitchen in Vitoria showcasing seasonal ingredients with refined technique and elegant plating.
restaurants.mustOrder: Piquillo peppers with goat cheese and jamón

restaurants.name: Las Cubanas
restaurants.description: Lively tapas bar beloved by locals for creative pintxos, croquetas, and a vibrant atmosphere in central Vitoria.

restaurants.name: Elkano
restaurants.description: Beachfront institution in Getaria famous for its txuleta de cherne (grilled fish) and stunning coastal views.

restaurants.name: Andra Mari
restaurants.description: One-Michelin-star seafood specialist with daily catches prepared simply to highlight pristine flavors.
restaurants.mustOrder: Grilled turbot with local Txakoli wine

restaurants.name: La Caja Mágica
restaurants.description: Intimate restaurant in medieval Hondarribia serving creative, elegant dishes in a charming 16th-century setting.
restaurants.mustOrder: Langostino a la sal con aguacate

restaurants.name: Sebastián
restaurants.description: Casual neighborhood gem in the historic quarter offering authentic home-style Basque cooking and warm hospitality.

restaurants.name: Alameda
restaurants.address: Calle Ramón y Cajal, 2, San Sebastián
restaurants.description: One-Michelin-star restaurant in modern Gros neighborhood focused on seasonal products and precise technique.
restaurants.mustOrder: Hake with cider sauce and apple

restaurants.name: Casa Montaña
restaurants.description: Historic pintxos bar and institution since 1957 where locals queue for exquisite small bites and vermouth.
restaurants.mustOrder: Jamón ibérico pintxo with manchego cheese

regions/galicia.json.name: Galicia
regions/galicia.json.history: Galicia, located in the northwestern corner of the Iberian Peninsula, has a history reaching back to Celtic settlements and Roman occupation. During the Middle Ages, it became a Christian stronghold and pilgrimage destination, particularly as the site of Santiago de Compostela, one of Christianity's holiest places. The region later fell under various kingdoms and eventually became part of the unified Spanish crown in the 15th century. Over the centuries, Galicia developed a distinct cultural identity shaped by its Atlantic maritime heritage, Celtic influences, and periods of relative isolation.
regions/galicia.json.culturalNotes: Galicia is renowned for its deep connection to the sea, reflected in its seafood cuisine, fishing traditions, and maritime festivals that celebrate local heritage. The region has a strong Celtic cultural legacy visible in its traditional music, bagpipes (gaitas), and folk dances that remain central to local celebrations and identity. Galician pride in regional distinctiveness is evident in its language, festivals like the Festa da Virxe da Barca, and the enduring pilgrimage routes that continue to draw visitors seeking spiritual connection.
regions/galicia.json.bestTimeToVisit: Summer is best for pleasant weather along the Atlantic coast and pilgrimage routes.
regions/galicia.json.languageNotes: Galician (Galego) is the co-official language alongside Spanish and is spoken by most locals in daily life; it shares Celtic roots and has similarities to Portuguese. Useful phrases include "Olá" (hello), "Gracias" (thank you), and "Fala galego?" (Do you speak Galician?), which locals appreciate hearing from visitors.

places.name: Santiago de Compostela
places.description: The spiritual capital of Galicia and endpoint of the Camino de Santiago pilgrimage, famed for its immense granite cathedral said to hold the relics of Saint James. Its atmospheric old town of narrow lanes, monasteries and lively squares is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

activities.detail: Marvel at the immense granite cathedral housing the crypt believed to hold the relics of Saint James, and see the botafumeiro incense censer swing during special masses.
Stand in the grand square in front of the cathedral's baroque facade, the traditional finish line for pilgrims completing the Camino de Santiago.
Learn the history of the Camino de Santiago pilgrimage routes and their cultural impact at this dedicated museum in the old town.
Wander the bustling stone-built food market to see fresh Galician seafood, cheeses and produce sold by local vendors.
Stroll through this leafy park for classic postcard views back toward the cathedral spires over the old town rooftops.
Explore one of Spain's largest monasteries, with an ornate church and museum just steps from the cathedral.

activities.text: Rúa do Franco and old town lanes
activities.detail: Wander the atmospheric granite streets lined with taverns, pilgrim shops and historic facades in the UNESCO-listed old quarter.

food.detail: Try Galicia's iconic octopus dish, boiled and sliced onto a wooden plate with paprika, sea salt and olive oil.
Sample the savory Galician pie filled with tuna, meat or seafood in a golden bread crust, sold by the slice in bakeries.
Finish a meal with this traditional almond cake dusted with powdered sugar in the shape of the Cross of Saint James.
Indulge in barnacles and other fresh shellfish pulled from the nearby Galician coast, served in old town marisquerías.
Taste the mild, creamy cone-shaped Galician cheese, often paired with quince paste as a simple dessert or tapa.
Pair your meal with a crisp local white wine, traditionally served in small ceramic bowls called cuncas in old-style taverns.

culture.detail: Many visitors are Camino pilgrims carrying a stamped credential, so be respectful of the emotional significance of arriving at the cathedral.
The daily Pilgrim's Mass at the cathedral is open to all visitors and occasionally features the famous botafumeiro swinging overhead.
Santiago is known for frequent rain, so carry an umbrella or light raincoat even in warmer months.
Shops and some restaurants may close or run limited hours on Sundays, so plan sightseeing and meals accordingly.

places.name: A Coruña
places.description: A vibrant Atlantic port city known for its glass-fronted galerías, sweeping promenade and the Tower of Hercules, the oldest working Roman lighthouse in the world. Its old quarter, beaches and lively tapas scene make it a rewarding coastal stop.

activities.detail: Ascend the 2nd-century Roman lighthouse, still in use today, for sweeping Atlantic views from its rocky headland park.
Walk or cycle along one of Europe's longest seaside promenades, lined with the city's famous glass galería facades.

places.name: Vigo
places.description: Galicia's largest city sits on a broad estuary and is a gateway to the Cíes Islands, blending a working port atmosphere with excellent seafood and a historic old town. It offers a lively, less touristy taste of urban Galician life.

activities.detail: Take the summer ferry from Vigo's harbor to this pristine archipelago with turquoise water and white-sand beaches inside the Atlantic Islands National Park.
Explore Vigo's old town with its granite alleys, tapas bars, and the lively Rúa Pescadería known locally as the 'Ostrería'.
This hilltop fortress park offers sweeping views over the Ría de Vigo and the city's port.
A charming arcaded square in the old town, popular for morning coffee and people-watching.

activities.text: Stroll the working port and marina
activities.detail: See Vigo's real identity as one of Europe's busiest fishing ports, with trawlers unloading alongside pleasure yachts.
Housed in a former prison, this museum showcases rotating contemporary art exhibitions in a striking building.
Wander the historic quarter where fishermen once lived, now filled with seafood restaurants near the old fish market.

food.detail: Buy fresh oysters shucked to order by the traditional 'ostreiras' (oyster women) right on the street known as 'A Pedra'.
Galicia's famed mussels, often served steamed in their own broth or 'a la marinera' with a tomato-wine sauce.
Tender boiled octopus sliced onto a wooden plate, dusted with paprika and coarse salt, a Galician classic.
A savory pie filled with tuna, cockles, or pork, sold by the slice in local bakeries and markets.
Goose barnacles harvested from Galicia's rocky coast, a pricey but prized local delicacy.
Pair your seafood with this crisp, aromatic white wine from the nearby Rías Baixas region.

culture.text: Book Cíes ferry and permits early
culture.detail: Access to the Cíes Islands is limited in summer, so ferry tickets and the required free park permit should be arranged in advance.
Like elsewhere in Spain, lunch is typically after 2pm and dinner after 9pm, so plan accordingly.
The ostreiras at A Pedra are a local institution—buying a few oysters and chatting is a genuine Vigo experience.
Vigo is less polished than Santiago or San Sebastián, offering an authentic, unpretentious slice of Galician daily life.

places.name: Cíes Islands
places.description: A protected archipelago in the Ría de Vigo often called one of the world's best beaches, with turquoise waters, dunes and dramatic cliffs. Access is limited and by ferry only, preserving its pristine, uncrowded feel.

activities.detail: Walk the powdery white sand and turquoise shallows of this beach often ranked among the world's best.
Climb the steep path to the island's highest point for sweeping views over the Ría de Vigo and Atlantic.
A shorter, family-friendly climb offering dramatic cliff-and-ocean panoramas on Monteagudo island.
Slip into the calm, transparent water near Rodas or Figueiras to spot fish among the rocks.
A quieter beach on the northern island reached by a scenic coastal trail from the ferry dock.
Observe one of Europe's largest yellow-legged gull colonies nesting on the island's cliffs.

activities.text: Cross the sandbar to Praia das Cantadoiras
activities.detail: Follow the natural sand spit connecting Rodas to a wilder, more secluded beach.
Stay overnight at the island's single designated campsite behind Rodas beach, open only in summer with advance booking.

food.detail: Tender boiled octopus sprinkled with paprika and olive oil, a Galician classic best enjoyed back in Vigo before or after your trip.
A savory Galician pie filled with tuna, mussels or pork, ideal for packing as a beach picnic since food options on the islands are minimal.
Fresh mussels steamed simply, reflecting the rich shellfish tradition of the surrounding Ría de Vigo.
A prized, pricey Galician delicacy harvested from the region's rocky Atlantic coastline.
A crisp, aromatic white wine from the nearby Rías Baixas region, perfect alongside seafood.
The islands' only eatery, offering simple grilled fish and drinks for day-trippers with no other food outlets nearby.

culture.text: Book ferry and entry permits ahead
culture.detail: Daily visitor numbers are capped (around 1,800-2,200), so ferry tickets and the required island access permit must be reserved online in advance.
There are no ATMs and only one small shop/bar, so pack water, snacks, sunscreen and cash for the day.
The islands are a protected natural park, so walking off designated trails or onto dunes is prohibited to preserve fragile ecosystems.
There is minimal waste infrastructure, and visitors are expected to carry all rubbish back with them on the ferry.

places.name: Lugo
places.description: A historic inland city encircled by the best-preserved and only complete Roman walls in the world, a UNESCO World Heritage Site you can walk along in full. Its compact old town offers charming plazas, cathedrals and traditional taverns.

activities.detail: Circle the 2,117-metre, 2nd-century Muralla de Lugo, the only completely intact Roman wall circuit in the world, for panoramic views over the old town and Miño valley.

activities.text: Visit the Cathedral of Santa María
activities.detail: Explore this Romanesque-Baroque cathedral with its Chapel of Corpus Christi, where the Blessed Sacrament is displayed continuously as a rare Vatican privilege.
Sit beneath the plane trees of Lugo's grand main square, lined with cafés and framed by the Concello and Iglesia de San Pedro.
Peek into the ruins of the ancient thermal baths beside the river, evidence of Lugo's origins as the Roman settlement of Lucus Augusti.
Housed in the former Convent of San Francisco, this museum displays Roman mosaics, Galician ceramics and local archaeology.
Stroll through this lively medieval square with its central fountain, a hub for tapas bars and street life in the old town.
Follow the paths and Roman bridge over the Río Miño just outside the walls for a quieter, green escape from the old town.

food.detail: Order a drink at almost any bar in Lugo and you'll receive a generous free tapa, a beloved local custom rare elsewhere in Spain.
Tender boiled octopus sliced onto wooden plates, dressed with paprika, olive oil and coarse salt, a Galician classic found on many menus.
A hearty broth of greens, potatoes, white beans and often pork, perfect for Lugo's cooler inland climate.
Savory pie filled with tuna, cockles or pork, sold by the slice in local bakeries and taverns.
Cured pork shoulder simmered with turnip greens and potatoes, a traditional inland Galician dish especially popular around Lugo.
A dense almond cake dusted with powdered sugar in the shape of St. James's cross, ideal with coffee after a meal.

culture.text: Order a drink to eat well
culture.detail: Since tapas usually come free with drinks in Lugo, locals treat bar-hopping in the old town as an affordable way to sample many dishes.

culture.text: Walk the wall clockwise or counter
culture.detail: There's no fixed direction for walking the Roman walls, but early morning or evening offers the best light and fewer crowds.
Many small shops and some restaurants close in the early afternoon, reopening later for dinner service around 9pm.
Signage and conversation often mix Galician and Spanish, and locals appreciate visitors attempting a few Galician words like 'grazas'.

places.name: Cape Finisterre
places.description: Once believed to be the end of the known world, this dramatic headland with its lighthouse marks the traditional finishing point for many Camino de Santiago pilgrims. Sunsets over the wild Atlantic here are legendary.

activities.detail: Walk out to this working lighthouse perched on the rocky point once thought to be the edge of the world.
Join pilgrims and travelers gathering at the cliffs near the lighthouse to watch the sun sink into the Atlantic, often applauded by the crowd.

activities.text: Burn your boots or clothes ritual
activities.detail: Follow the old pilgrim tradition of burning a worn item of clothing or boots at the cape to symbolize the end of the Camino, though it's now discouraged for fire safety.

activities.text: Explore Praia de Mar de Fóra
activities.detail: Visit this wild, often wind-battered beach just below the cape, known for dramatic waves and a moody, end-of-the-world atmosphere.

activities.text: Hike the coastal path from Fisterra town
activities.detail: Walk the scenic 3km route from the fishing village of Fisterra up to the lighthouse, passing shrines and viewpoints along the way.

activities.text: Visit the Ermida de Nosa Señora das Areas
activities.detail: Stop at this small chapel on the way to the cape, tied to local maritime devotion and pilgrim tradition.
Photograph the iconic 0.00 km stone marker signaling the symbolic end of the Camino de Santiago.
Wander this small 18th-century fort in Fisterra town, now home to a modest maritime and fishing museum.

food.detail: Galicia's classic boiled octopus dish, sliced and dressed with olive oil, paprika, and coarse salt, widely available in Fisterra's restaurants.
A prized and pricey local delicacy harvested from the rocky, wave-battered coastline near the cape.
A hearty Galician broth made with greens, potatoes, and pork, perfect after a windy walk along the headland.
A savory seafood-filled pastry pie typical of coastal Galician towns like Fisterra.

food.text: Fresh grilled fish at the port
food.detail: Sample the day's catch, often hake or sardines, grilled simply and served at harborside restaurants in Fisterra town.
A traditional almond cake dusted with powdered sugar in the shape of the St. James cross, a fitting sweet finish for pilgrims.

culture.detail: Many walkers perform symbolic acts here like burning clothes or collecting a shell, so be mindful and respectful of these quiet personal moments.
The cape is famously windy and exposed even in summer, so pack accordingly regardless of the season.
Arrive at least an hour before sunset to find parking and a good spot, as the headland gets crowded at golden hour.
Many pilgrims extend their walk from Santiago to Fisterra and then on to Muxía, so trail markers and albergues are common in the area.

places.name: Ribeira Sacra
places.description: A stunning river canyon region where the Sil and Miño rivers carve deep gorges lined with ancient terraced vineyards and secluded monasteries. Boat trips on the Sil Canyon reveal breathtaking views of this dramatic landscape.

activities.detail: Take a catamaran cruise from Doade or Los Peares along the Sil Canyon to see terraced vineyards clinging to near-vertical slopes.
Wander the three cloisters of this former Benedictine monastery, now a parador, hidden deep in the gorge.
Take in one of the most photographed panoramas of the Sil Canyon from this cliffside lookout near Parada de Sil.
Visit one of Spain's oldest monastic sites, carved directly into the rock in the 6th century.
Follow footpaths through the socalcos, the ancient stone terraces where grapes are still harvested by hand.

activities.text: Bodega visit in the Amandi zone
activities.detail: Tour a small family winery near Sober to taste Mencía grown on some of Galicia's steepest slopes.
Explore the medieval Torre del Homenaje and Baroque Colegio del Cardenal in the region's main town.
Discover this quiet, half-ruined Romanesque monastery tucked into the woods above the Sil.

food.detail: Mineral, elegant red wines grown on impossibly steep river-facing terraces, a designation of origin worth tasting at source.
Tender boiled octopus dressed with paprika and olive oil, a Galician classic found in local taverns.
A prized seasonal delicacy, this river lamprey is traditionally stewed in its own blood with wine and onions.
A savory pie filled with tuna, pork, or seafood, sold by the slice in village bakeries.

food.text: Queixo de San Simón da Costa
food.detail: A smoked, pear-shaped cow's milk cheese from nearby Lugo province, often paired with local honey.
A dense almond cake dusted with powdered sugar in the shape of the Cross of Saint James.

culture.detail: The vineyards here are so steep they must be worked entirely by hand, a practice locals call 'viticultura heroica'.
Sil Canyon cruises run on limited schedules and sell out quickly in summer, so reserve in advance.
Dozens of monasteries and hermitages were built here by monks who first planted the region's vineyards centuries ago.
Cliffside paths and viewpoints can be uneven, and canyon weather shifts quickly between sun and mist.

places.name: Combarro
places.description: A picturesque fishing village on the Ría de Pontevedra celebrated for its iconic hórreos (granite granaries) built right along the waterfront. Its narrow stone streets and seafood restaurants make it a favorite postcard stop.

activities.detail: Stroll along the granite granaries perched on rocks right at the water's edge, Combarro's most photographed sight.
Lose yourself in the tangle of narrow stone lanes like Rúa do Mar and Rúa da Fábrica lined with old fishermen's houses.
Look for the weathered stone crosses scattered through the village, remnants of centuries-old Galician religious tradition.

activities.text: Visit the Church of San Roque
activities.detail: This small parish church anchors the historic quarter and is a good landmark for exploring on foot.

activities.text: Take a boat trip on the ría
activities.detail: Hire a small boat to see the mussel-farming rafts (bateas) floating offshore and views of Combarro from the water.

activities.text: Relax at Praia de A Chousa
activities.detail: A small nearby beach good for a quiet swim or picnic after touring the village.

activities.text: Photograph the sunset over the estuary
activities.detail: The waterfront promenade offers one of the best sunset views along the Ría de Pontevedra.
Visit the nearby Monastery of San Xoán de Poio with its cloisters and gardens overlooking the ría.

food.detail: Tender boiled octopus sliced and dressed with paprika, olive oil and coarse salt, a Galician classic served everywhere here.
Fresh mussels farmed on the floating rafts visible just offshore, usually steamed or served in escabeche.
Prized goose barnacles harvested from the rocky Galician coast, a local delicacy worth splurging on.
