places.description: A small provincial capital famous for its extraordinary Mudejar architecture, recognized by UNESCO, including ornately decorated towers. It's also home to the legendary tragic love story of the Lovers of Teruel.

activities.text: Climb the Torre de El Salvador
activities.detail: Ascend this ornate 13th-century Mudejar minaret-tower for close-up views of its glazed tilework and panoramic views over the city.

activities.text: See the Torre de San Martín
activities.detail: Admire this UNESCO-listed twin tower, one of Teruel's finest examples of Mudejar brickwork and ceramic decoration.

activities.text: Visit the Catedral de Santa María de Mediavilla
activities.detail: Step inside to see the famous artesonado wooden ceiling, painted with medieval scenes and considered a masterpiece of Mudejar art.

activities.text: Explore the Mausoleo de los Amantes de Teruel
activities.detail: Visit the alabaster tombs and multimedia exhibit dedicated to the legendary star-crossed lovers Diego and Isabel.
Stroll through the lively main square, home to the tiny bull statue that has become the symbol of Teruel.
Explore this large paleontology theme park celebrating the rich dinosaur fossil finds of the Teruel region.
Descend or climb this Neo-Mudejar decorated stairway linking the old town to the train station, adorned with ceramic tiles and sculpture.
See the Mudejar church tower and cloister that form part of the Lovers of Teruel monument complex.

food.detail: Sample this DOP-protected dry-cured ham, aged in the region's cold mountain air and prized across Spain.
Try this tender roast lamb dish, a specialty of Aragonese cuisine often slow-cooked with herbs and potatoes.
Taste this hearty dish of fried breadcrumbs, garlic, and chorizo, traditionally eaten by shepherds in the region.
Look for dishes featuring the region's prized black truffle, harvested locally in winter.
Enjoy this traditional sweet pastry filled with nuts and honey, often served during local festivals.
Pair your meal with a robust red wine from the nearby Bajo Aragón wine region.

culture.detail: Many shops and some sights close for a few hours in the early afternoon, so plan visits accordingly.

culture.text: Time your visit for Las Bodas de Isabel de Segura
culture.detail: This February festival reenacts the medieval legend of the Lovers of Teruel with parades and period costumes throughout the old town.
Teruel's compact historic center means most Mudejar sights can be seen comfortably on foot in a day.
Teruel's ham carries its own Denominación de Origen, so locals take pride in how and where it's served.

places.name: Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park
places.nameIt: Parque Nacional de Ordesa y Monte Perdido
places.description: A spectacular Pyrenean national park with glacial valleys, towering limestone cliffs, and cascading waterfalls. Hiking trails here rival the Alps in grandeur, drawing nature lovers from across Europe.

activities.detail: A roughly 17km round-trip trail from the Pradera de Ordesa car park leads through beech forest and cascades to this iconic horsetail waterfall at the head of the valley.

activities.text: Walk the Faja Pelay balcony trail
activities.detail: This high, narrow ledge path offers dramatic views down into the Ordesa valley and across to the sheer limestone walls of the Fisco face.
A series of stepped travertine cascades along the Río Arazas make for one of the most photogenic stops on the classic Ordesa circuit.
This narrower, wilder canyon on the park's southern edge features plunging gorges and hanging footbridges over turquoise water.

activities.text: Drive into the Valle de Pineta
activities.detail: A glacially carved valley on the park's northern flank, reachable by road, with Monte Perdido's glacier visible at its head near Bielsa.
Experienced mountaineers can summit this 3,355m limestone giant, usually overnighting at the Refugio de Góriz.
Keep an eye on the rocky slopes above the valley trails, where herds of these agile mountain goats are commonly seen grazing.

activities.text: Visit the Bujaruelo valley and medieval bridge
activities.detail: A quieter approach to the high Pyrenees, this valley features an old stone pilgrim bridge and access routes toward the French border.

food.detail: Tender roast lamb from a protected Aragonese breed, often slow-cooked with rosemary and served in village restaurants near the park.
A hearty shepherd's dish of fried breadcrumbs mixed with garlic, chorizo, and grapes, popular in the mountain villages around Torla and Broto.
River trout from the Ara or Cinca rivers, pan-fried with cured ham, a classic Pyrenean mountain meal.
Firm, tangy Aragonese sheep's or mixed-milk cheeses often served as a starter with local honey or membrillo.
A cured, slightly smoky pork sausage from the nearby Ribagorza region, sliced thin as tapas.
A nearby wine region producing robust reds and aromatic whites that pair well with hearty mountain fare.

culture.text: Book the summer shuttle bus early
culture.detail: In peak season access to the Pradera de Ordesa is restricted to a bus from Torla, so arrive early or reserve tickets in advance.
Pyrenean weather can change fast even in summer, so bring layers, waterproofs, and sturdy boots regardless of season.

culture.text: Reserve mountain refuges ahead of time
culture.detail: Huts like Refugio de Góriz fill up quickly in summer, so book bunks and meals well before your trek.

culture.text: Stay on marked trails and respect wildlife
culture.detail: The park protects fragile alpine flora and species like the bearded vulture, so drones and off-trail hiking are prohibited.

places.name: Alquézar
places.description: A stunning cliffside village crowned by a fortified medieval collegiate church overlooking the Vero River gorge. It's a gateway for canyoning adventures and scenic riverside walkways carved into rock.

activities.text: Colegiata de Santa María la Mayor
activities.detail: Explore this fortified medieval collegiate church perched on a cliff edge, with its Gothic cloister and faded Romanesque frescoes.
Walk the wooden and metal walkways bolted into the gorge walls beneath the village for dramatic views up at the cliffside houses.

activities.text: Canyoning in the Sierra de Guara
activities.detail: Join a guided descent through nearby canyons like La Peonera or Cueva Cabrera, rappelling past waterfalls and natural pools.

activities.text: Mirador de la Cruz de la Ilesia
activities.detail: Take in the classic postcard view of Alquézar's church and cliffs from this lookout point across the gorge.
Get lost in the narrow cobbled lanes and stone archways of the medieval quarter below the main square.

activities.text: Ermita de la Virgen del Viñedo
activities.detail: Visit this small hermitage a short walk from the village surrounded by vineyards and olive groves.

food.detail: Try this protected-origin roast lamb, a signature dish of the region often slow-cooked with local herbs.
Sample fried breadcrumbs cooked with garlic, pork, and grapes, a hearty shepherd's dish typical of the Somontano area.
Taste wines from the nearby Somontano DO, known for its mix of native and international grape varieties grown at the foot of the Pyrenees.
Look for handmade cured sausages sold in village shops, often flavored with local mountain herbs.
Seek out sheep's milk cheeses from the nearby village of Radiquero, a small-scale regional specialty.

culture.detail: The riverside walkways and old town streets are steep, uneven, and slippery in places, so sturdy shoes are essential.
Sierra de Guara canyoning trips fill quickly in summer, so reserve with a local outdoor company ahead of time.

culture.text: Respect quiet hours in the old town
culture.detail: Many houses in the historic center are still lived in, so keep noise down in the evenings out of respect for residents.

culture.text: Visit early or late in summer
culture.detail: Midday heat and tourist crowds peak in July and August, so plan sightseeing for morning or early evening.

places.name: Monasterio de Piedra
places.description: A 12th-century monastery surrounded by a lush nature park filled with waterfalls, grottoes, and lakes fed by the Piedra River. The dramatic Cola de Caballo waterfall is a highlight of this unexpected oasis.

activities.detail: Marvel at this dramatic 50-meter horsetail waterfall, the park's signature natural spectacle formed by the Piedra River.
Walk behind the cascading water into this atmospheric cave illuminated by shifting light and rainbow mist.
Stroll beside this glassy 'Mirror Lake' that perfectly reflects the surrounding cliffs and greenery.
Tour the Cistercian cloister, chapter house, and refectory that give the park its historic name.
Visit the monastery's original bodega, now a museum tracing centuries of monastic winemaking on the estate.
Cross this stone footbridge for classic views over the river gorge and surrounding woodland.

activities.text: Hike the full nature park circuit
activities.detail: Follow the marked trail past lakes, grottoes, and cascades on a roughly two-hour walking loop through the gorge.

food.detail: Try this protected-origin roast lamb, a staple of Aragonese cuisine often served in nearby Nuévalos restaurants.
Sample the hearty fried breadcrumb dish traditionally cooked with chorizo, bacon, and grapes.
Enjoy freshly caught river trout, a local specialty given the abundance of clean water in the park.
Taste wines linked to the monks' historic winemaking tradition, still celebrated in the old cellar.
Finish with this crunchy Aragonese almond nougat, a classic regional sweet.

culture.detail: Paths near the waterfalls and grottoes can be wet, uneven, and involve many stairs.
The park draws large crowds from Zaragoza and Madrid, so arriving at opening time avoids the busiest hours.
The former monastery building now operates as a hotel, letting visitors experience the site after day-trippers leave.
Mist from the waterfalls can dampen clothing and camera equipment even on sunny days.

places.name: Sos del Rey Católico
places.description: A well-preserved medieval hilltop village and birthplace of King Ferdinand II of Aragon, encircled by ancient walls and a castle. Its stone streets and noble mansions transport visitors back centuries.

activities.text: Castillo de Sos and the Torre del Homenaje
activities.detail: Climb this restored hilltop castle keep for sweeping views over the Cinco Villas plain and the village's red-tiled rooftops.
Visit the noble stone palace where King Ferdinand II of Aragon was born in 1452, now home to a small interpretive exhibit.
Explore this fortified Romanesque church with a frescoed crypt and a portal richly carved with biblical scenes.
Stroll the narrow lanes of the old Jewish quarter, lined with stone houses and archways dating to the Middle Ages.

activities.text: Puerta de la Reina and the town walls
activities.detail: Pass through the medieval gate that still guards the old town and trace the surviving stretches of defensive wall.

activities.text: Plaza de la Villa and the Lonja
activities.detail: Sit beneath the arcaded medieval market hall in the main square, the social heart of the village since the 13th century.
Walk to this lookout point for panoramic views of the Sierra de la Peña and surrounding countryside.

food.detail: Tender roast suckling lamb, a protected regional specialty typically slow-roasted with garlic and herbs.
Fried breadcrumbs cooked with chorizo, bacon and grapes, a hearty shepherd's dish common in Aragonese villages.
Fresh white beans stewed with partridge, a rustic dish reflecting the hunting traditions of the Cinco Villas region.
A cured, spiced pork sausage often grilled or added to stews, sold in local butcher shops.
Sharp sheep's milk cheeses from nearby Navarre and Aragón hillsides, often served as a tapa with local wine.
Sample robust reds and rosés from small regional bodegas, best paired with roast lamb.

culture.detail: The village's steep cobbled streets and stairways demand comfortable, grippy footwear.
The Palacio de Sada and castle keep limited visiting hours, especially off-season, so confirm times locally before planning your day.
Many stone houses are still private homes, so keep noise down and stay on public streets and paths.
Time your trip for the town's medieval market or September fiestas honoring King Ferdinand, when streets fill with period costume and music.

places.name: Aínsa
places.description: A charming Pyrenean town with a beautifully preserved medieval quarter and a grand cobbled main square. Its hilltop position offers sweeping views of surrounding valleys and mountains.

activities.detail: This grand porticoed square, paved with irregular stones and lined with sandstone houses, is the heart of the medieval town and hosts markets and festivals.

activities.text: Visit the Colegiata de Santa María
activities.detail: Climb the Romanesque bell tower for panoramic views and descend into the atmospheric crypt beneath the 11th-century church.
Walk the ramparts of this 11th-century fortress, once a defensive stronghold against Moorish incursions, for sweeping views over the Sobrarbe valleys.
Wander narrow cobbled streets between stone houses with wooden balconies, many now housing artisan shops and small galleries.
From the town's hilltop edge, look out over the confluence of the Cinca and Ara rivers with the Pyrenees rising behind.
This interpretation center explains the region's geology, wildlife, and mountain culture, a good primer before heading into Ordesa National Park.

activities.text: Day trip to Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park
activities.detail: Just a short drive away, this UNESCO-listed park offers dramatic canyons, waterfalls, and high-mountain trails.
Small shops around the old town sell handmade leather goods, ceramics, and local textiles reflecting Pyrenean craft traditions.

food.detail: Slow-roasted Aragonese lamb, a regional specialty often served with potatoes in mountain restaurants around town.
Freshly caught river trout, typically pan-fried with almonds or cured ham in the local style.
Wild mushrooms foraged from the surrounding forests, sautéed simply with garlic and parsley in autumn menus.

food.text: Queso de Radiquero o del Sobrarbe
food.detail: Artisan sheep's or goat's cheese from nearby Pyrenean villages, often served as a starter with local honey.
Cured sausages and cold meats from mountain pig-farming traditions, sold in local delis and served as tapas.
A traditional Aragonese dessert of fried batter wrapped around a borage leaf, dusted with sugar.

culture.detail: Restaurants typically serve lunch from 1:30 to 3:30pm and dinner from 8:30 or 9pm onward, later than in northern Europe.
The historic center is largely pedestrian and cobbled, so visitors usually leave cars in designated lots below the hill and walk up.
Aínsa is a small town where shops may close for a midday siesta, especially outside peak summer season.
The town hosts a lively Medieval Festival each September with markets, music, and period costumes filling the Plaza Mayor.

places.name: Benasque
places.description: A picturesque mountain town in the high Pyrenees, surrounded by some of Spain's tallest peaks including Aneto. It's a premier base for hiking, climbing, and skiing in dramatic alpine scenery.

activities.detail: Tackle Spain's highest peak outside the Canaries via the Renclusa refuge and a short glacier crossing on the Pyrenees' most classic summit day.
Stroll cobbled lanes lined with slate-roofed stone mansions and the fortified Palacio de los Condes de Ribagorza.
Ride Aragon's highest ski resort in winter or use its lifts for high-altitude summer hiking with views over the valley.
Relax in a small thermal spa set beside a rushing mountain river deep in the forested upper valley.
Follow a long alpine trail to a striking turquoise glacial lake ringed by 3,000m peaks in Posets-Maladeta Natural Park.

activities.text: Visit Iglesia de Santa María Mayor
activities.detail: See this Romanesque-Gothic church, one of the valley's oldest religious buildings, right in the town center.
Test yourself on one of several protected climbing routes threading cliffs and gorges around the valley.

activities.text: Drive the Vallibierna or Estós valleys
activities.detail: Take a scenic side road or trail into quieter side valleys full of pine forest, streams, and grazing cattle.

food.detail: Tender roast lamb from a protected Aragonese breed, usually oven-cooked with rosemary and potatoes.
Fresh river trout from the valley's cold mountain streams, often pan-fried with cured ham.
Wild mushrooms foraged in the surrounding beech and pine forests, served grilled or in hearty stews each autumn.
A traditional Pyrenean dessert of borage leaves dipped in batter, fried, and dusted with sugar.
Shepherd-style fried breadcrumbs with garlic, chorizo, and grapes, a filling dish rooted in mountain herding life.
Local cured sausages and hams from the valley, often paired with mountain cheese as a hiker's lunch.

culture.detail: Many valley residents speak Benasqués, a distinct Aragonese-Occitan dialect, alongside Spanish.
Stop by the Parque Natural Posets-Maladeta information office for trail, weather, and avalanche updates before heading into the high peaks.
Small shops and some restaurants often close for a few hours in early afternoon, especially outside peak season.
Mountain refuges like La Renclusa expect early booking, quiet hours, and minimal water use during busy summer climbing season.

places.name: Valle de Hecho
places.description: A remote and scenic Pyrenean valley known for its traditional stone villages, dense forests, and the eerie rock formations of the Selva de Oza. It offers a quieter, unspoiled alternative to more crowded mountain areas.

activities.detail: Wander the streets of Hecho village where the Museo de Escultura al Aire Libre scatters modern stone and iron sculptures among the old stone houses.

activities.text: Visit the Monastery of San Pedro de Siresa
activities.detail: This austere Romanesque monastery in the nearby village of Siresa was once a key stop on a branch of the Camino de Santiago.

activities.text: Walk through the Selva de Oza
activities.detail: Hike among the dense beech and fir forest of Selva de Oza, where weathered limestone crags called Los Agallones rise eerily above the trees.

activities.text: Peer into the Boca del Infierno gorge
activities.detail: A short trail leads to this dramatic narrow limestone canyon carved by the Aragón Subordán river.
A rewarding high mountain trail near the French border ends at this glacial lake ringed by peaks.

activities.text: Seek out the Dolmen de Ibirque
activities.detail: This Neolithic megalithic tomb sits quietly in the countryside, a reminder of the valley's ancient human presence.

activities.text: Stay a night at Refugio de Gabardito
activities.detail: This mountain refuge is a popular base for exploring the Sierra de Alano and spotting griffon vultures overhead.

food.detail: A cured, spiced pork sausage typical of the valley, often grilled or added to stews.
Slow-roasted young Aragonese lamb, a classic mountain dish served in local restaurants.
Wild mushrooms foraged from the valley's forests appear in autumn menus, often sautéed with garlic and jamón.
A firm, tangy sheep's or mixed-milk cheese made in the valley, best paired with local honey or membrillo.
Freshly caught mountain trout, usually pan-fried with ham, is a staple of valley cuisine.
A hearty shepherd's dish of fried breadcrumbs with chorizo, garlic, and grapes, rooted in the region's pastoral traditions.

culture.detail: Traditional stone houses in Hecho and Siresa feature odd conical chimney caps once believed to ward off witches.
Shops, fuel stations, and restaurants are sparse and often close outside peak season, so plan ahead.
Sheep and cattle are still driven along mountain paths seasonally, so give herds and shepherds the right of way.

culture.text: Time your visit for local fiestas
culture.detail: Hecho's early August festival brings traditional jota dancing, regional dress, and communal meals to the village square.

restaurants.name: El Chalet
restaurants.description: Elegant restaurant showcasing contemporary interpretations of traditional Aragonese cuisine with seasonal menus.

restaurants.name: La Pepica
restaurants.description: Family-run establishment famous for grilled meats and local Somontano wines in a rustic setting.

restaurants.name: Casa Julio
restaurants.address: Calle Don Juan de Austria 16, Zaragoza
restaurants.description: Historic tavern established in 1955 serving authentic local specialties in a convivial atmosphere.

restaurants.name: Marengo
restaurants.description: Contemporary restaurant featuring refined presentations of regional ingredients with creative techniques.
restaurants.mustOrder: Jamón de Teruel with local cheese

restaurants.name: Los Braseros
restaurants.description: Cozy mountain restaurant specializing in hearty Pyrenean fare and local game dishes.
restaurants.mustOrder: Trucha a la navarra (trout Navarre style)

restaurants.name: El Tubo
restaurants.description: Lively traditional tapas street packed with historic bars offering small plates and local wines.
restaurants.mustOrder: Jamón ibérico (Iberian ham) with Pan con tomate

restaurants.name: Vega
restaurants.description: Innovative plant-based restaurant offering creative vegetable dishes in a minimalist setting.

restaurants.name: Casa Emilio
restaurants.description: Family restaurant preserving traditional recipes and local Huesca ingredients since 1970.

restaurants.name: Almohada 10
restaurants.description: Sophisticated venue offering modern Spanish dishes with carefully sourced regional products.

restaurants.name: La Bota de Pepe
restaurants.description: Historic wine bar with excellent selection of Aragonese wines paired with quality cured meats and cheeses.
restaurants.mustOrder: Queso de cabra with local wine pairing

restaurants.name: Casa Pascualillo
restaurants.description: Charming village restaurant in medieval Albarracín serving hearty local cuisine in stone-walled rooms.

restaurants.name: Amparito Roca
restaurants.description: Legendary tapas bar in El Tubo quarter celebrated for its jamón ibérico and classic Aragonese bites.

restaurants.name: Restaurante Peña
restaurants.description: Traditional restaurant highlighting Teruel's culinary heritage with emphasis on jamón and local specialties.

restaurants.name: Casa Marcial
restaurants.description: Fine dining establishment fusing Pyrenean traditions with contemporary culinary techniques and Somontano wines.
restaurants.mustOrder: Wild mushroom and game tasting menu

regions/canary_islands.json.name: Canary Islands
regions/canary_islands.json.capital: Las Palmas de Gran Canaria / Santa Cruz de Tenerife
regions/canary_islands.json.history: The Canary Islands were inhabited by the Guanche people before Spanish conquest began in the late 14th century, with full colonization completed by the early 16th century. The islands became a crucial strategic and commercial hub for Spanish maritime expansion into the Atlantic and the New World. Throughout the centuries, the archipelago faced numerous pirate attacks and raids due to its strategic location and wealth, requiring the construction of fortified settlements. The islands remained largely agricultural until the 20th century, when tourism transformed them into one of Spain's most important economic zones.
regions/canary_islands.json.culturalNotes: The Canaries blend Spanish, North African, and Latin American influences, creating a unique cultural identity distinct from mainland Spain. Local traditions include distinctive folk music and dance like the timple guitar music, colorful carnival celebrations rivaling those of Rio de Janeiro, and a strong maritime heritage reflected in local cuisine and customs. The island communities are known for their relaxed, welcoming atmosphere and outdoor lifestyle, with year-round mild weather facilitating a culture centered on beaches, festivals, and social gatherings.
regions/canary_islands.json.bestTimeToVisit: Year-round destination thanks to a mild subtropical climate, best in winter for northern escapees.
regions/canary_islands.json.languageNotes: While Spanish is the official language, the local dialect features distinctive pronunciation and vocabulary influenced by historical contact with Portuguese and Moorish cultures. Useful phrases include 'Hola, que tal' for greeting and 'Muchas gracias' for thanks, though English is increasingly common in tourist areas.

places.name: Teide National Park
places.description: Home to Spain's highest peak, Mount Teide, a dormant volcano rising dramatically from a surreal lunar landscape of lava fields and craters. A cable car and hiking trails let visitors explore terrain so otherworldly it has doubled for Mars in film shoots.

activities.detail: A cable car climbs from 2,356m to nearly 3,555m in about eight minutes, giving quick access to near-summit views without a full day hike.
With a free permit, hikers can trek the final stretch from the cable car's upper station to Teide's 3,718m summit for panoramic views over the Canary Islands.
Walk the short circular trail around these dramatic volcanic rock formations, including the iconic Roque Cinchado, framed by Teide in the background.

activities.text: Cross the Llano de Ucanca plain
activities.detail: Stroll across this vast, flat volcanic plain that resembles a dried lakebed, ringed by the eroded walls of the ancient Las Cañadas caldera.
Teide's high altitude and clear air make it one of the world's best stargazing spots, with guided night tours and telescope viewing available.
This popular route climbs through pumice slopes and offers acclimatization before continuing on to the summit refuge and crater.

activities.text: Visit the El Portillo Visitor Centre
activities.detail: Learn about the park's unique volcanic geology and endemic plants like the red tajinaste through exhibits and a short botanical trail.

food.detail: Small wrinkled potatoes boiled in salted water and served with spicy red mojo picón or green mojo verde, a Canarian staple found at park-area restaurants.
Local goat cheese from mountain herds grazing near the park, often smoked or aged, served as a starter or with mojo.
A hearty stew of pasta, chickpeas, potatoes, and meat, popular as warming mountain fare in the cooler highland climate around Teide.
Toasted ground grain (gofio) is mixed into soups, stews, or desserts, reflecting traditional Canarian highland cuisine.

food.text: Volcanic wines from Tenerife's high vineyards
food.detail: Vines grown in volcanic soil on Teide's slopes produce distinctive mineral-driven whites and reds, available for tasting in nearby wineries.

culture.text: Get a summit permit in advance
culture.detail: Access to the final stretch above the cable car's upper station requires a free permit obtainable online, often booked out weeks ahead in high season.
Temperatures and weather can shift drastically between the base and summit, so warm clothing is essential even in summer.
At over 2,000m altitude, sun intensity is much higher, so sunscreen, hats, and sunglasses are strongly recommended.
The park protects fragile endemic species like the Teide violet, so visitors are asked to avoid stepping off designated paths.

places.name: San Cristobal de La Laguna
places.description: A UNESCO World Heritage town with a well-preserved historic center of colorful colonial buildings and grid-pattern streets that inspired many Latin American cities. Its university atmosphere and beautifully restored churches make it a cultural highlight of Tenerife.
imageQueries: San Cristobal de La Laguna street

activities.detail: The town's central square is lined with historic buildings including the Convent of Santa Catalina and the Cabildo, making it the perfect starting point for a walk.

activities.text: Visit the Cathedral of La Laguna
activities.detail: This neoclassical cathedral holds the remains of conquistador Alonso Fernández de Lugo and features an eclectic mix of architectural styles.

activities.text: Wander the Iglesia de la Concepción
activities.detail: One of the oldest churches on Tenerife, its Mudejar-style tower and interior reflect the island's early colonial religious architecture.

activities.text: Stroll Calle de la Carrera and Calle San Agustín
activities.detail: These grid-pattern streets, lined with colorful colonial facades and wooden balconies, were the urban model later exported to Latin American cities.

activities.text: Tour the Museo de Historia y Antropología (Casa Lercaro)
activities.detail: Housed in a 16th-century mansion, this museum traces the island's history from conquest to modern times.

activities.text: See the University of La Laguna's historic building
activities.detail: Founded in 1792, the university gives the town its lively student atmosphere and hosts a small museum on its history.
An elegant 18th-century palace often used for cultural exhibitions, showcasing Canarian aristocratic architecture.
A lively local market for fresh produce, cheeses, and Canarian products, good for picking up picnic supplies or souvenirs.

food.detail: Small wrinkled potatoes boiled in salty water and served with red or green mojo sauce, a Canarian staple found in local restaurants.
A hearty watercress and vegetable stew often enriched with pork, typical of traditional Canarian home cooking.

food.text: Carne de cabra o conejo en salmorejo
food.detail: Goat or rabbit marinated in a spiced garlic sauce, a rustic dish reflecting the island's rural culinary roots.
Toasted grain flour used in soups, desserts, or mixed with milk, a Canarian ingredient with centuries of history.
A dense Canarian-style flan made with condensed milk, popular as a dessert in local eateries.
La Laguna sits near vineyards producing volcanic-soil wines; many bars in town offer tastings of local reds and whites.

culture.detail: The historic center is pedestrianized and best explored slowly, as its street grid inspired colonial town planning across the Americas.
Many churches close for midday hours and expect modest dress from visitors.
Thanks to the university, La Laguna has a lively bar and tapas scene, especially around Calle Herradores in the evenings.

culture.text: Visit on market or festival days
culture.detail: Check for local festivities like the Bajada de la Virgen or weekend markets, which bring extra life to the plazas.

places.name: Masca
places.description: A dramatic mountain village perched in the Teno massif, famous for its steep gorge hike leading down to a secluded cove. Its remote, rugged setting offers some of Tenerife's most spectacular views.

activities.detail: A steep, dramatic 3-4 hour descent through a rocky gorge ending at a secluded pebble cove, considered one of Spain's most spectacular hikes.
Stop at this roadside viewpoint on the TF-436 for a jaw-dropping panorama of the village clinging to the ravine walls.
Explore Masca's tiny stone-and-thatch hamlets connected by narrow paths carved into the terraced hillside.

activities.text: Visit Iglesia de San Antonio Abad
activities.detail: See the small whitewashed parish church that anchors the village's central square.
After the gorge hike, catch a boat from the black-sand cove across to Los Gigantes or Puerto de Santiago.
Take the hair-raising, hairpin mountain road for views over the Teno massif and the Atlantic beyond.

food.detail: A hearty Canarian watercress and vegetable stew often served at village cafés as a hiker's refuel.
Wrinkled salt-boiled potatoes served with spicy red mojo picón or green mojo verde sauce.
Local goat cheese from herds grazing the surrounding Teno massif, often grilled or served with palm honey.
Toasted grain flour used in soups, breads, and desserts, a staple of traditional Canarian cuisine found in Masca's small eateries.
A layered coffee with condensed milk, liqueur, and cinnamon, worth trying at one of the village's mirador cafés.

culture.text: Book a hiking permit in advance
culture.detail: Access to the Barranco de Masca trail is limited and requires a free permit reserved online through the Teno Rural Park system.
The gorge trail is rocky, narrow, and can be slippery, so sturdy footwear is essential.
Masca's tiny lanes get congested with tour buses, so arriving early morning or using the shuttle bus is recommended.
Sudden rain can make the gorge dangerous due to flash flooding, so trail conditions should be confirmed locally.

places.name: Los Gigantes
places.description: A resort town named for the towering sea cliffs that plunge dramatically into the Atlantic beside it. Boat trips from its marina offer chances to spot dolphins and whales against a stunning cliffside backdrop.

activities.detail: Take a catamaran or small boat from Los Gigantes marina to see the sheer cliff face from the water and spot resident pilot whales and dolphins.
Relax on this small black volcanic sand beach that sits right at the foot of the towering cliffs.
Stroll the harbor, browse boat trip kiosks, and watch fishing boats unload beside the yachts.
Drive a short distance to Adeje for this dramatic gorge trail leading to a waterfall, one of Tenerife's best-known hikes.
Head to this popular black-sand beach in nearby Puerto de Santiago for swimming and sunbathing with cliff views.
Dive centers here offer trips to underwater volcanic reefs and caves right beneath the towering Acantilados de los Gigantes.

activities.text: Sunset viewpoint at Mirador de la Hondura
activities.detail: Catch golden-hour light hitting the cliff faces from this scenic overlook above town.
Tandem paragliding flights launch from nearby hills for aerial views of the cliffs and Atlantic coastline.

food.detail: Small wrinkled potatoes boiled in salty water and served with spicy red mojo picón or green mojo verde sauce, a Canarian staple found in every local restaurant.
Try this local parrotfish, a Canarian favorite, grilled simply and served with mojo at harborside seafood restaurants.
Marina restaurants serve the day's catch, often cherne or sama, grilled or baked whole with olive oil and garlic.
Sample dishes made with gofio, a toasted grain flour unique to the Canary Islands, used in soups, desserts, and as a bread substitute.
Grilled local goat cheese served with mojo sauce makes a popular tapa in Los Gigantes eateries.
Finish a meal with this layered Tenerife specialty coffee made with condensed milk, liqueur, cinnamon, and lemon zest.

culture.text: Book whale watching trips with certified operators
culture.detail: Look for boats displaying the Blue Boat certification to ensure responsible, low-impact wildlife viewing in this protected marine area.
Restaurants typically don't fill up until 8pm or later, following mainland Spanish dining customs.
The Atlantic sun and coastal winds here are stronger than they feel, so use sunscreen even on cloudy days.
The Acantilados de los Gigantes are a protected natural monument, so stick to marked paths and viewpoints rather than climbing on the rock faces.

places.name: Las Palmas de Gran Canaria
places.description: Gran Canaria's bustling capital combines a golden urban beach, Las Canteras, with a charming old quarter of colonial buildings in Vegueta. It's a lively mix of history, culture, and beach life in one compact city.
imageQueries: Las Palmas Gran Canaria Las Canteras beach

activities.text: Sunbathe at Playa de Las Canteras
activities.detail: This nearly 3km golden urban beach has a natural rock barrier (La Barra) creating calm waters perfect for swimming and beginner surfing.
Stroll the cobbled streets of the city's founding neighborhood, lined with colonial-era mansions, wooden balconies, and quiet plazas.
This atmospheric museum, set in a former governor's residence, explores Columbus's stopover in the Canaries en route to the Americas.

activities.text: See the Catedral de Santa Ana
activities.detail: Climb the bell tower of this centuries-old cathedral for sweeping views over Vegueta's rooftops and out to the Atlantic.
This lively covered market is a great spot to sample local produce, cheeses, and grab a casual lunch among locals.
This palm-lined square near the port is the social heart of the Puerto district, surrounded by cafes and buzzing with life, especially during Carnival.

activities.text: Explore Pueblo Canario and Museo Néstor
activities.detail: This recreated traditional village showcases Canarian architecture and houses artwork by island painter Néstor Martín-Fernández de la Torre.

activities.text: Walk out to Castillo de La Luz
activities.detail: This 16th-century fortress once guarded the port entrance and now sits as a restored landmark near the marina.

food.detail: Small wrinkly potatoes boiled in salty water and served with red mojo picón or green mojo verde sauce, a Canarian staple found everywhere.
A hearty stew of shredded meat, chickpeas, and vegetables, often served as a homestyle lunch special in Vegueta's traditional restaurants.
A traditional dish of salted fish (often grouper or sea bass) served with potatoes, sweet potato, and mojo sauce.

food.text: Queso de flor and Canarian cheeses
food.detail: Try local artisan cheeses, some made with thistle flower rennet, often served with mojo or palm honey as a starter.

food.text: Fresh seafood in Puerto de Las Palmas
food.detail: The area near the port and Playa de Las Canteras has excellent seafood restaurants serving the day's catch grilled simply with olive oil.
Look for gofio, a toasted grain flour unique to the Canaries, used in everything from desserts to savory dishes and even ice cream.

culture.detail: Lunch typically starts around 2pm and dinner rarely before 8:30-9pm, so plan accordingly to dine when locals do.
Las Palmas hosts one of the world's largest carnival celebrations in February, transforming the city with parades, costumes, and street parties.
Las Canteras looks calm but currents can vary along its length, so pay attention to posted flags and lifeguard advice.
Some smaller shops in Vegueta and Triana still observe a midday closure, reopening later in the afternoon.

places.name: Maspalomas Dunes
places.description: An iconic expanse of golden sand dunes stretching along the southern coast of Gran Canaria, resembling a desert beside the sea. The adjacent resort area and lighthouse make it a top destination for sun-seekers and photographers alike.

activities.detail: Wander the shifting golden sand peaks near the Maspalomas Lighthouse, especially striking in the soft evening light.
This 19th-century lighthouse marks the eastern edge of the dunes and offers a classic photo backdrop.
This protected freshwater lagoon within the dune reserve attracts herons, coots, and migratory species.
Time your visit for sunset when the dunes glow amber and cast dramatic shadows across the sand.

activities.text: Beach walk to Playa del Inglés
activities.detail: Follow the coastline where the dunes meet the sea, connecting Maspalomas beach to the lively Playa del Inglés.
Take a short guided ride through the dune fringes for a traditional desert-style experience, availability varies by season.

food.detail: Small wrinkled potatoes served with spicy red mojo picón and herby green mojo verde, a Canarian classic found at nearby beach restaurants.
Chiringuitos along Playa del Inglés serve the day's catch simply grilled with olive oil and sea salt.
A traditional Canarian dish made from toasted gofio grain mixed with fish broth, worth trying at a local eatery near the dunes.

food.text: Queso de flor with local honey
food.detail: A Gran Canaria cheese made partly with thistle flower rennet, often paired with island honey as a starter.

food.text: Sunset cocktail at a dune-view terrace
food.detail: Many beachfront bars near the dunes serve drinks with panoramic views as the sun sets over the sand.
A sweet almond and honey treat popular across the Canary Islands, often served after a seaside meal.

culture.detail: Certain stretches of the dunes and adjoining beach are clothing-optional by local custom, so be aware of designated areas.
The dunes are a protected natural reserve, so walking only on designated trails helps preserve the fragile ecosystem.
Early morning or late afternoon visits avoid the intense sun and offer the best light for photos.
The nearby Playa del Inglés area is known for its inclusive, LGBT-friendly bars and nightlife scene.

places.name: Puerto de Mogán
places.description: A picturesque harbor town nicknamed 'Little Venice' for its flower-draped bridges and canals lined with whitewashed houses. Its relaxed charm and weekly market make it a favorite escape from Gran Canaria's larger resorts.

activities.detail: Wander over the small footbridges draped in bougainvillea that link the canal-side walkways, giving the town its 'Little Venice' nickname.
Join locals and visitors at the weekly street market along the harbor selling crafts, leather goods, spices and souvenirs.
Spend time on this sheltered, family-friendly beach with golden imported sand right next to the marina.

activities.text: Watch the boats in the marina
activities.detail: Walk along the yacht marina lined with cafes to watch fishing boats and pleasure craft come and go.

activities.text: Take a boat trip to see dolphins
activities.detail: Book a catamaran or small boat excursion from the harbor for a chance to spot dolphins and whales offshore.
Follow the barranco trail inland from the port into palm-filled valleys and traditional Canarian villages.

food.detail: Try the catch of the day, often vieja (parrotfish) or cherne, grilled simply and served at harborside restaurants.
Wrinkled Canarian potatoes served with spicy red mojo picón or green mojo verde sauce, a staple side dish.
A traditional salted fish stew with potatoes, sweet potato and mojo sauce, popular in local eateries.
Many waterfront restaurants serve generous seafood paellas perfect for sharing while watching the boats.
Sample local goat cheese and gofio, a toasted grain flour used in soups, desserts and as a bread substitute.

culture.text: Visit on Fridays for the market
culture.detail: The main street market only runs on Friday mornings, so plan your visit accordingly if you want to shop.
Puerto de Mogán is relaxed, but swimwear should be reserved for the beach and pool areas rather than restaurants.
Rounding up the bill or leaving a small tip in restaurants is customary but not obligatory in Spain.

culture.text: Expect a quieter pace than nearby resorts
culture.detail: Unlike Playa del Inglés or Puerto Rico, Mogán retains a slower, traditional fishing-village atmosphere, especially in the evenings.

places.name: Roque Nublo
places.description: A towering volcanic rock formation rising from the highlands of Gran Canaria, sacred to the island's ancient inhabitants. The hike to its base rewards visitors with sweeping views across the island's mountainous interior.

activities.text: Hike to the base of Roque Nublo
activities.detail: An easy-to-moderate trail from the La Goleta parking area climbs gently for about an hour through pine forest and volcanic scrubland to reach the foot of the towering monolith.

activities.text: Stand beneath the sacred rock itself
activities.detail: Up close, the 80-meter basalt monolith reveals the wind and rain erosion that shaped it over millions of years, and its neighboring formation Roque de la Rana.

activities.text: Continue to Pico de las Nieves
activities.detail: A short drive or extended hike leads to Gran Canaria's second-highest peak, offering 360-degree views that stretch to Tenerife's Mount Teide on clear days.
This nearby sacred rock, once an aboriginal ceremonial and astronomical site, pairs well with Roque Nublo for a fuller picture of Gran Canaria's pre-Hispanic culture.

activities.text: Watch the sunset paint the rock gold
activities.detail: Late afternoon light turns the volcanic stone deep orange and red, making it one of the island's best sunset-viewing spots.

activities.text: Explore Degollada de la Goleta viewpoint
activities.detail: This mountain pass near the trailhead offers sweeping views over Gran Canaria's rugged caldera landscape before or after the hike.
One of Spain's officially recognized 'most beautiful villages,' Tejeda sits below the trailhead and makes a charming base with mountain views.

food.detail: A rich almond, honey, and egg yolk dessert closely associated with Tejeda, the village at the foot of Roque Nublo.
Chewy almond cookies made from locally grown almonds, sold in bakeries throughout the village.
A hearty watercress and vegetable stew typical of Gran Canaria's cooler highland villages, perfect after a mountain hike.

culture.detail: Roque Nublo was a place of ritual significance for the island's pre-Hispanic Guanche inhabitants, so visitors should avoid climbing on the rock itself and stay on marked paths.
Weather in the highlands shifts quickly, with fog, wind, and sun often appearing within the same short hike.
Arriving in the morning helps you beat both the crowds and the midday heat, and secures a parking spot at the small La Goleta lot.
Small bakeries and family-run restaurants in Tejeda and Ayacata often prefer cash over card payments.

places.name: Corralejo
places.description: A lively beach town on Fuerteventura known for its dazzling white sand dunes and turquoise waters, popular with surfers and kitesurfers. It's also the ferry gateway to the pristine islet of Lobos.

activities.detail: Wander through this protected natural park of shifting white sand dunes that stretch along the coast right next to town.

activities.text: Day trip to Isla de Lobos
activities.detail: Hop on a short ferry from the harbor to this tiny volcanic islet with crystal-clear lagoons and walking trails.

activities.text: Surf or kitesurf at Flag Beach
activities.detail: Head to this well-known beach just north of town, famous for consistent wind and waves that draw surfers and kitesurfers year-round.

activities.text: Stroll the old harbor and marina
activities.detail: Wander the original fishing port area, now lined with cafes, boats, and views back toward Lobos and Lanzarote.

activities.text: Snorkel or dive the volcanic reefs
activities.detail: Explore clear waters and volcanic rock formations just offshore, home to colorful fish and occasional rays.

activities.text: Family fun at Baku Water Park
activities.detail: Spend a day on slides and pools at this small water park geared toward kids and families in the town center.

food.detail: Try grilled vieja (parrotfish) or other catch-of-the-day fish served simply with olive oil at harborside restaurants.
Small wrinkly salted potatoes served with spicy red mojo picón or green mojo verde sauce, a Canarian staple.
A distinctive goat cheese from Fuerteventura, often served as a starter with local honey or mojo.
Sample gofio, a toasted grain flour used in soups, desserts, and as a traditional accompaniment to stews.
Many seafront restaurants serve generous seafood paellas perfect for sharing after a day at the beach.

culture.detail: Some beaches near Corralejo have powerful winds and currents, so pay attention to flag warnings before swimming.
Locals and restaurants often don't serve dinner until 8 or 9pm, in line with typical Spanish dining hours.

culture.text: Respect the dunes and marine park
culture.detail: Stick to marked paths in the natural park and avoid removing sand, plants, or marine life as souvenirs.

culture.text: Book Lobos ferries ahead in high season
culture.detail: Ferry tickets to Isla de Lobos can sell out in summer, so reserve in advance if you want to visit.

places.name: Betancuria
places.description: Fuerteventura's tranquil former capital, tucked in a valley surrounded by rugged mountains, with a historic church and small museums. Its quiet streets offer a glimpse into the island's colonial and religious history.

activities.text: Iglesia de Santa María de Betancuria
activities.detail: Explore this fortress-like 18th-century church, rebuilt after pirate raids, with a beautifully carved wooden ceiling and religious artifacts.
Housed in a former convent, this small museum displays centuries-old religious art and vestments from island churches.
Learn about the Majos, Fuerteventura's original Guanche inhabitants, through pottery and tools on display.

activities.text: Ruins of Convento de San Buenaventura
activities.detail: Wander the atmospheric stone ruins of a 15th-century Franciscan monastery destroyed by pirates.
Drive up to this dramatic viewpoint just outside town for sweeping views over Betancuria's valley and volcanic peaks.
Wander cobbled lanes lined with whitewashed colonial-era houses that reveal the island's oldest settlement.

food.detail: Sample this prized Fuerteventura goat cheese, often served with local honey or mojo sauce.
Try tender goat meat slow-cooked in a rich sauce, a traditional dish reflecting the island's pastoral heritage.
Taste this toasted grain flour, a Canarian staple used in everything from soups to desserts.
Warm up with this hearty watercress and vegetable stew common in rural Fuerteventura kitchens.
Look for red and green mojo condiments served with potatoes or meats at local eateries.

culture.detail: Betancuria is quiet and small, with many shops and museums closing for a midday break.
Cover shoulders and knees when visiting the historic Iglesia de Santa María de Betancuria.
Museums and sites often have restricted hours, so check ahead, especially outside peak tourist season.
As Fuerteventura's first capital founded in 1404, the town holds deep historical pride for locals.

places.name: Teguise
places.description: Lanzarote's atmospheric former capital, a well-preserved colonial town famous for its Sunday market and cobbled streets lined with historic mansions. Its rich history and artisan crafts reflect the island's traditional culture.

activities.text: Sunday Market in Plaza de la Constitución
activities.detail: Wander stalls of local crafts, produce and live folk music that take over the town's cobbled streets every Sunday morning.
Climb Montaña Guanapay to this 16th-century volcanic-stone fortress, now home to the quirky Pirate Museum.
Tour this beautifully restored 18th-century mansion showcasing traditional Canarian architecture and period furnishings.

activities.text: Iglesia de Nuestra Señora de Guadalupe
activities.detail: Visit the whitewashed parish church at the heart of town, rebuilt after pirate raids and fires over the centuries.
Explore this former convent turned cultural space hosting art exhibitions and craft demonstrations.
Watch local luthiers hand-craft the timple, the small five-stringed guitar central to Canarian folk music.

food.detail: Wrinkled salt-boiled potatoes served with spicy red mojo picón or green mojo verde sauce.
Try this toasted grain flour, a Canarian staple, served as a dessert, drink or savory accompaniment.
A hearty stew of noodles, chickpeas, potatoes and meat traditionally eaten on Sundays.
Sample tangy goat cheese from the islands drizzled with palm or cane honey, sold at the Sunday market.
Graze on grilled meats, empanadas and fresh juices from vendors set up around the Sunday market.

culture.detail: The market gets extremely crowded by late morning, so come early to enjoy it before tour buses arrive.
Cover shoulders and knees when entering the Iglesia de Nuestra Señora de Guadalupe or the convent.

culture.text: Expect a midday lull on weekdays
culture.detail: Many shops and restaurants close for a few hours in the early afternoon outside of market day.
Prices at craft stalls are generally fixed and fair, reflecting the town's long tradition of handmade goods.

places.name: Timanfaya National Park
places.description: A dramatic volcanic landscape formed by 18th-century eruptions, featuring reddish-black terrain, craters, and geothermal demonstrations. Guided bus tours and camel rides reveal this Martian-like 'Fire Mountain' scenery unique in Europe.

activities.text: Route of the Volcanoes bus tour
activities.detail: A guided bus ride is the only way to access the park's dramatic interior, winding through craters and lava fields shaped by the 1730s eruptions.
Rangers demonstrate the earth's residual heat by pouring water into a pipe to produce an instant geyser and igniting dry brushwood placed just below the surface.
Designed by César Manrique, this restaurant cooks meats over a grill powered entirely by geothermal heat rising from the volcanic vents beneath it.

activities.text: Camel ride up the volcanic slopes
activities.detail: At the Echadero de los Camellos, dromedaries carry visitors on a short ascent through the ash-covered Islote de Timanfaya cones.
The Centro de Visitantes e Interpretación explains the park's volcanic origins, ecology, and the history of the eruptions that buried nearby villages.
The Montañas del Fuego offer surreal, Mars-like vistas of red, black, and ochre volcanic cones stretching to the coastline.

food.text: Geothermally grilled meats at El Diablo
food.detail: Chicken, pork, and sausages are cooked directly over natural volcanic heat instead of charcoal or gas, a novelty unique to this restaurant.
Small wrinkled potatoes boiled in salty water and served with spicy red mojo or green herb mojo, a Canarian staple found at park-area eateries.
Toasted grain flour, a pre-Hispanic Canarian food, appears in soups, desserts, and as a side to local stews near Timanfaya.
Nearby vineyards grow vines in volcanic ash pits, producing distinctive mineral-driven Malvasía wines often paired with lava-cooked meals.
Lanzarote's volcanic pastures yield tangy goat cheeses often served as a starter before exploring the park.

culture.text: No independent walking or driving into the core zone
culture.detail: To protect the fragile volcanic ecosystem, visitors must explore the central park by official bus tour or on a restricted guided walking route.
Entry slots for the bus tour and restaurant can sell out quickly during peak season, so reserving ahead is strongly advised.

culture.text: Dress for wind and sun exposure
culture.detail: The exposed lava terrain offers little shade and can be windy, so hats, sunscreen, and layers are recommended even on mild days.
Straying off designated paths damages slow-forming volcanic soils and lichen colonies that are still recovering after nearly 300 years.

places.name: Jameos del Agua
places.description: A striking cave and cultural venue created by artist César Manrique inside a collapsed volcanic tunnel, featuring a natural lagoon and concert space. It's a masterful blend of natural wonder and artistic design unique to Lanzarote.
imageQueries: Cesar Manrique Jameos del Agua cave

activities.detail: Admire the crystal-clear turquoise pool inside the collapsed lava tube, home to the famous tiny blind albino crabs (jameitos).

activities.text: Visit the Casa de los Volcanes auditorium
activities.detail: Explore the surreal concert hall carved into the volcanic rock, renowned for its extraordinary natural acoustics.

activities.text: Walk through the tropical garden terraces
activities.detail: Stroll among Manrique's landscaped white walls, black volcanic rock, and lush palms that blend architecture with nature.

activities.text: Explore the House of the Volcanoes exhibition
activities.detail: Learn about Lanzarote's volcanic geology and the island's unique cave ecosystems through interactive displays.
Look closely into the lagoon's dark waters to find the rare, translucent crabs unique to this cave system.
Check for evening concerts or performances held in the cave, when the space takes on a magical, otherworldly atmosphere.

food.detail: Try Lanzarote's iconic wrinkled potatoes served with spicy red mojo picón or green mojo verde at the on-site restaurant.
Sample locally caught fish such as vieja or cherne, simply grilled and seasoned with island herbs.
Taste traditional Canarian gofio (toasted grain flour) used in mousses or ice cream for a distinctly local flavor.
Pair your meal with a glass of Lanzarote's volcanic-soil malvasía wine, grown just a short drive from the site.

culture.detail: Keep noise levels low near the lagoon, as sound carries strongly through the volcanic tunnel and can disturb the crabs and other visitors.
Flash photography is discouraged near the lagoon to protect the sensitive, light-averse blind crabs living there.

culture.text: Visit early or late for fewer crowds
culture.detail: Jameos del Agua is a major tour bus stop, so arriving at opening time or in the late afternoon offers a more peaceful experience.

culture.text: Remember it's a protected natural site
culture.detail: The cave and its ecosystem are protected, so swimming in the lagoon and touching the rock formations are strictly prohibited.

places.name: San Sebastián de La Gomera
places.description: The historic port town where Columbus made his final stop before crossing the Atlantic, retaining colonial-era buildings and a charming waterfront. It serves as the gateway to La Gomera's lush, dramatic interior.
imageQueries: San Sebastian de La Gomera port
San Sebastian de La Gomera old town

activities.detail: Visit this squat 15th-century fortress, the oldest surviving military building in the Canary Islands, set in a palm-shaded park by the sea.
Step into the church where Columbus is said to have prayed before his 1492 voyage, noted for its mix of Gothic and Mudejar details.
See the well from which Columbus's ships supposedly drew their last water before crossing the Atlantic.
Browse this small museum and cultural center dedicated to Columbus's connection with the island and its Atlantic history.

activities.text: Stroll the Calle Real and waterfront
activities.detail: Wander the pedestrian old-town streets lined with colonial facades down to the harbor promenade for views of arriving ferries.
Relax on the town's dark volcanic-sand beach, a convenient in-town spot for a swim after sightseeing.

activities.text: Day trip into Garajonay National Park
activities.detail: Use the town as a base to drive up into the misty ancient laurel forests of this UNESCO-listed park in the island's interior.
Pause at this palm-fringed square, a hub of local life ringed by cafés and municipal buildings.

food.detail: Try this spicy spreadable pâté of aged Gomeran cheese, garlic, and red pepper, usually served with bread as a starter.
Sample small wrinkled potatoes boiled in salted water and served with red mojo picón or green mojo verde sauce.
Warm up with this hearty watercress and vegetable stew typical of Gomeran home cooking.
Look for this dark palm syrup, a local specialty drizzled over desserts or gofio, unique to La Gomera's palm groves.
Finish a meal with this dense local flan-style dessert, a Canarian take on caramel custard.

food.text: Fresh grilled fish at the harbor
food.detail: Eat just-landed vieja or cherne at a portside restaurant, often accompanied by papas arrugadas.

culture.detail: San Sebastián's daily life revolves around the Tenerife ferry schedule, so expect brief bursts of activity around arrivals and departures.
Locals take pride in the town's role in 1492, so visit the Torre del Conde and Pozo de la Aguada with a bit of historical reverence.
Many small shops and some restaurants close for a few hours in the early afternoon, especially outside peak tourist season.
La Gomera moves at a relaxed tempo compared to Tenerife, so allow extra time and patience for service and transport connections.

restaurants.name: El Límite
restaurants.address: Calle Pérez Galdós 6, Santa Cruz de Tenerife
restaurants.description: Michelin-starred restaurant offering innovative takes on Canarian and Spanish cuisine with impeccable service.

restaurants.name: Casa Montesdeoca
restaurants.address: Calle Montesdeoca 10, Las Palmas de Gran Canaria
restaurants.description: Award-winning fine dining restaurant in a historic colonial building serving creative Mediterranean cuisine.

restaurants.name: El Patio
restaurants.address: Calle Rubén Darío 4, Puerto de la Cruz, Tenerife
restaurants.description: Charming family-run restaurant in a restored colonial house featuring authentic Canarian cuisine and local wines.

restaurants.name: Vega
restaurants.address: Calle Vegueta 13, Las Palmas de Gran Canaria
restaurants.description: Innovative vegetable-forward tasting menu restaurant with artistic plating and creative flavor combinations.

restaurants.name: La Tranca
restaurants.description: Casual, atmospheric tapas bar in Tenerife's historic university town with excellent local wines.

restaurants.name: Cueva Pintada
restaurants.address: Calle Isabel de Castilla 2, Gáldar, Gran Canaria
restaurants.description: Traditional seafood restaurant overlooking the coast, known for fresh local catches and authentic recipes.

restaurants.name: Risco
restaurants.address: Calle Leoncio Rodríguez 23, Santa Cruz de La Palma
restaurants.description: Hillside restaurant with panoramic views serving traditional Canarian and Spanish dishes with local ingredients.

restaurants.name: Artemisia
restaurants.address: Calle Fuerteventura 5, Puerto del Rosario, Fuerteventura
restaurants.description: Contemporary restaurant blending Mediterranean and Canarian influences with focus on local fish and seafood.
restaurants.mustOrder: Fresh catch of the day with local herbs

restaurants.name: Restaurante La Tasquita de Enfrente de Råpelas
restaurants.address: Calle Pérez Galdós 16, Santa Cruz de Tenerife
restaurants.description: Lively tapas bar with creative small plates and an excellent selection of Spanish wines and sherries.
restaurants.mustOrder: Pan con tomate with jamón ibérico

restaurants.name: Casa Perico
restaurants.description: Beachfront institution serving fresh seafood and traditional Canarian specialties in a relaxed family atmosphere.

restaurants.name: El Charcón
restaurants.address: Calle Real 39, San Sebastián de La Gomera
restaurants.description: Historic family restaurant with colonial charm serving traditional Canarian dishes and fresh local fish.

restaurants.name: El Rincón de Juan Carlos
restaurants.description: Sophisticated restaurant with Michelin recognition serving modern Spanish cuisine with Canarian influences.
restaurants.mustOrder: Local grouper with tropical fruit sauce

restaurants.name: Bimbache
restaurants.description: No-frills local favorite serving authentic island home cooking and fresh seafood at budget-friendly prices.

restaurants.name: Balneario
restaurants.description: Beachfront casual dining with excellent fresh fish, paella, and sea views perfect for relaxed meals.

regions/balearic_islands.json.name: Balearic Islands
regions/balearic_islands.json.history: The Balearic Islands have been inhabited since prehistoric times and were colonized by Phoenicians, Greeks, and Romans, each leaving their mark on the archipelago. During the medieval period, the islands fell under Muslim rule for several centuries before being reconquered by Christian kingdoms in the 13th century. The strategic location made them frequent targets for pirate raids and naval conflicts, particularly during the 16th and 17th centuries. In more recent times, the islands developed into a major tourist destination during the 20th century, transforming their economy and culture while maintaining their distinct Mediterranean identity.
regions/balearic_islands.json.culturalNotes: The Balearic Islands possess a unique blend of Mediterranean and Spanish traditions, with a strong emphasis on local pride and regional autonomy. The islands are famous for their vibrant nightlife, pristine beaches, and picturesque villages that retain traditional stone architecture and whitewashed buildings. Local festivals like the Fiestas de Sant Joan showcase traditional music, dance, and culinary practices that have been preserved for centuries, while the islands' thriving artistic community continues to draw creative individuals from around the world.
regions/balearic_islands.json.bestTimeToVisit: Late spring to early autumn is best for beaches and nightlife.
regions/balearic_islands.json.languageNotes: Catalan, specifically the Balearic dialect (Mallorquí, Menorquí, and Ibicenc), is the co-official language alongside Spanish. Useful phrases include "Bon dia" (good morning), "Merci" (thank you), and "Adéu" (goodbye), though most residents and tourists manage well with Spanish or English.

places.name: Palma
places.description: The vibrant capital of the Balearic Islands, home to a stunning Gothic cathedral overlooking the sea. Its old town, tapas bars, and harborfront make it the cultural and culinary heart of Mallorca.

activities.detail: This soaring Gothic cathedral perched above the sea is Palma's most iconic landmark, famous for its rose window and Gaudí-influenced interior.
A rare circular medieval castle set on a hilltop, offering panoramic views over the city and bay.
Get lost in the narrow lanes around Carrer de Sant Miquel and Plaça de Cort, lined with Renaissance courtyards and boutique shops.
These small, atmospheric 10th-century Arab baths are among the few remaining traces of Palma's Moorish past.
A lively indoor market where locals shop for fresh seafood, cheese, and produce, with tapas bars scattered throughout.
A modern art museum built into old fortress walls, showcasing works by Miró and Picasso with sea views from its terraces.

activities.text: Stroll Passeig del Born and the harborfront
activities.detail: Palma's grand boulevard leads down to the marina, lined with yachts, palm trees, and elegant cafés.

food.detail: A light, spiral-shaped pastry dusted with powdered sugar, traditionally eaten for breakfast.
Rustic bread rubbed with tomato and olive oil, often topped with local cheese or cured meats.
A soft, spreadable cured sausage made with paprika, a Mallorcan specialty found on nearly every tapas menu.
A layered vegetable dish of potatoes, eggplant, and peppers baked in tomato sauce, similar to ratatouille.
This former fishermen's neighborhood is now packed with lively tapas bars serving both traditional and modern dishes.
A hearty fried dish of lamb offal, potatoes, and vegetables, seasoned with fennel and paprika.

culture.detail: Many small shops close for a few hours in the early afternoon, especially outside peak tourist season.
Shoulders and knees should be covered when visiting La Seu and other churches.
Locals typically eat dinner around 9pm or later, so restaurants may be quiet earlier in the evening.
On weekends, artisan stalls fill the square, a good spot for local crafts and people-watching.

places.name: Serra de Tramuntana
places.description: A dramatic UNESCO-listed mountain range stretching along Mallorca's northwest coast, with terraced villages and winding roads. It offers some of the best hiking, cycling, and scenery in the Mediterranean.

activities.detail: Follow this historic mule track built by Archduke Ludwig Salvator through pine forests with sweeping sea views between Valldemossa and Deià.

activities.text: Explore Sa Calobra and Torrent de Pareis
activities.detail: Drive the dramatic switchback road down to this cove where a spectacular limestone gorge meets the sea.
Stroll the honey-stone lanes of this artists' village once home to Robert Graves, ending at the cliffside cemetery overlooking the Mediterranean.

activities.text: Ride the vintage train to Sóller
activities.detail: Take the century-old wooden train from Palma through the mountains, then continue by antique tram to Port de Sóller.
Climb the ancient stone-paved ravine trail lined with terraced orchards, a classic Tramuntana hiking route.
Explore Mallorca's most important pilgrimage sanctuary, tucked in the mountains and home to the revered Moreneta statue.

activities.text: Tour the Royal Charterhouse in Valldemossa
activities.detail: Visit the monastery where Chopin and George Sand once wintered, filled with period rooms and courtyard gardens.
Pedal the legendary winding highway favored by professional cyclists, with hairpin bends and panoramic viewpoints like Mirador de Ses Barques.

food.detail: Sample the sweet citrus grown in the Sóller valley's terraced groves, often sold fresh at roadside stands or as juice.
Try this soft, paprika-cured pork sausage spread on bread, a staple of Mallorcan charcuterie.
Enjoy the light, spiral-shaped pastry dusted with powdered sugar, best with a morning coffee.
Order this simple rustic dish of bread rubbed with tomato and olive oil, often topped with local cheese or ham.
Taste the layered vegetable dish of potatoes, eggplant, and peppers baked in tomato sauce, a Mallorcan comfort food.
Finish a meal with this herbal digestif infused with local mountain herbs, served chilled.

culture.text: Drive the mountain roads with care
culture.detail: The MA-10 and village roads are narrow and full of hairpin turns, so allow extra time and watch for cyclists.
Many shops and restaurants in smaller villages close on Sundays and during the midday siesta hours.
Trails can be rocky and steep, so sturdy footwear and plenty of water are essential even for short walks.
Much of the land is private and cultivated, so stay on marked paths and close any gates you pass through.

places.name: Deia
places.description: A picturesque stone village clinging to the Tramuntana hillside, long favored by artists and writers like Robert Graves. Its cobbled lanes, hidden cove, and mountain backdrop make it one of Mallorca's most charming stops.

activities.detail: Climb the steep cobbled streets past golden stone houses draped in bougainvillea below the parish church.

activities.text: Visit Sant Joan Baptista church and cemetery
activities.detail: This hilltop church has a small cemetery with sweeping sea views where Robert Graves is buried, marked simply with his name.

activities.text: Tour Ca N'Alluny, the Robert Graves House
activities.detail: Explore the restored home and garden of the poet and novelist who made Deià an artists' haven from the 1930s onward.
Hike or drive down to this pebbly cove with turquoise water, backed by two rustic beach chiringuitos serving fresh fish.
Visit the Italianate mansion of Archduke Ludwig Salvator, with its marble belvedere overlooking the sea and Sa Foradada peninsula.

activities.text: Hike the Camí de sa Muntanya to Sóller
activities.detail: Follow this old mule path through terraced olive groves and pine forest along the Tramuntana ridge to the neighboring town.

activities.text: Watch the sunset from Sa Foradada viewpoint
activities.detail: Look out toward the natural rock arch jutting into the sea, best seen from the Son Marroig terrace at dusk.

activities.text: Browse the artisan galleries and boutiques
activities.detail: Pop into small studios and shops selling ceramics, paintings, and jewelry made by the village's long line of resident artists.

food.detail: Rustic bread rubbed with tomato and olive oil, topped with local cheese, sobrassada, or cured ham.
Soft, paprika-spiced cured sausage made from the native black Mallorcan pig, often served on toast.
A layered vegetable dish of fried eggplant, potato, and pepper baked in tomato sauce, a Mallorcan classic.

food.text: Fresh grilled fish at Cala Deià
food.detail: Order the catch of the day, often bream or grouper, at one of the small beach shacks overlooking the cove.
A coiled, powder-sugared pastry perfect with morning coffee at one of the village cafés.

food.text: Vi de la Terra de Mallorca wine
food.detail: Sample local wines from Tramuntana vineyards, often paired with tapas at Deià's terrace restaurants.

culture.text: Book restaurants ahead in high season
culture.detail: Deià is tiny and popular with visiting celebrities, so tables fill quickly in summer evenings.
Streets are narrow and parking is scarce, so use the small lots at the village edges and walk in.

culture.text: Dress modestly in the church and cemetery
culture.detail: The hilltop church remains an active place of worship, so cover shoulders and keep voices low.

culture.text: Respect the artistic, quiet character of the town
culture.detail: Many residents are working artists and writers, so keep noise down, especially in residential lanes at night.

places.name: Soller
places.description: A citrus-growing valley town connected to Palma by a vintage wooden train, with elegant Modernista architecture. Its nearby port and tram ride to the sea make it a beloved Mallorca excursion.

activities.text: Ride the vintage wooden train from Palma
activities.detail: The 1912 Ferrocarril de Sóller winds through tunnels and orange groves in the Tramuntana mountains on original wooden carriages.

activities.text: Take the antique tram to Port de Sóller
activities.detail: A rattling open-sided tram trundles from town to the seaside port along orange orchards and the coast.
This parish church's striking Modernista facade was designed by Joan Rubió, a disciple of Gaudí.
A restored Art Nouveau mansion showcasing period architecture alongside a collection of modern art.

activities.text: Stroll the harbor at Port de Sóller
activities.detail: This horseshoe-shaped bay offers a scenic promenade, swimming spots, and boat trips along the coast.
A classic cobbled mule path climbs through a dramatic gorge into the Tramuntana mountains, a UNESCO World Heritage range.

activities.text: Explore the Jardí Botànic de Sóller
activities.detail: This botanical garden preserves native Mallorcan and Balearic plant species in a peaceful valley setting.
Just a short drive away, this honey-stone village is often called one of Spain's prettiest, with steep lanes and mountain views.

food.text: Fresh orange juice from the plaza stalls
food.detail: Sóller's fertile valley is famous for its citrus, and vendors squeeze juice from local oranges right in the main square.
This spiral pastry dusted with powdered sugar is a Mallorcan classic, sometimes filled with local orange or pumpkin cream.
Orange-flavored ice cream or sorbet made with fruit from the surrounding groves is a local specialty.
Cured, spreadable pork sausage seasoned with paprika, traditionally served with honey and bread.
A rich lobster stew found at port restaurants, a splurge-worthy taste of Mallorcan seafood tradition.
Rustic bread rubbed with tomato and drizzled with olive oil, often topped with local cheese or cured ham.

culture.detail: The historic Palma-Sóller train is popular with tourists and midday departures often sell out in high season.

culture.text: Time your visit for Saturday market day
culture.detail: The main square and surrounding streets fill with produce, crafts, and local goods every Saturday morning.
Many small shops close for a few hours in the early afternoon in keeping with traditional siesta hours.

culture.text: Catch the Fira de Sa Fira in May
culture.detail: This agricultural fair celebrates the valley's citrus heritage with markets, food stalls, and local produce.

places.name: Cala Macarella
places.description: A turquoise cove on Menorca's south coast, framed by pine-covered cliffs and white sand. It's considered one of the most beautiful beaches in the Balearics, reachable by a scenic coastal path.

activities.detail: Take a dip in the strikingly turquoise, calm water framed by pine-covered limestone cliffs.
Follow the short cliffside path south to this smaller, quieter twin cove often favored by those seeking solitude.

activities.text: Hike a stretch of the Camí de Cavalls
activities.detail: Join Menorca's ancient coastal horse trail for dramatic clifftop views over both coves and the pine forest.
Explore the clear water along the cove's flanking rocks where small fish and sea grass meadows are visible.

activities.text: Watch the sunset from the cliffs
activities.detail: Climb to the pine-shaded viewpoint above the beach for golden-hour light over the turquoise water.
See the cove from the water, the classic way many visitors first admire its cliffs and coloring.

food.detail: Menorca's signature lobster stew, rich with tomato and often served at restaurants near Cala Galdana just inland.
Try the island's tangy, semi-cured cow's milk cheese, a Menorcan staple often served as tapas.
Soft cured pork sausage paired with honey, a classic Balearic combination found on local menus.
The Menorcan drink of local gin mixed with lemonade, refreshing after a hot walk to the beach.
A light, coiled pastry dusted with powdered sugar, popular for breakfast before a beach day.

culture.detail: Parking at the top of the access road fills quickly in summer, especially by mid-morning.
The beach has little natural shade and only a small seasonal snack bar, so pack supplies.

culture.text: Respect the protected dunes and pines
culture.detail: Stick to marked paths since the surrounding vegetation and dune system are environmentally protected.

culture.text: Boats must anchor away from posidonia meadows
culture.detail: Mooring restrictions protect the seagrass beds that keep the water clear, so swimmers may see marker buoys offshore.

places.name: Ciutadella
places.description: Menorca's former capital boasts a beautifully preserved old quarter with a Gothic cathedral, grand palaces, and a picturesque small port. Its summer fiestas and elegant plazas give it a refined, historic charm.

activities.detail: A fortress-like 14th-century Gothic cathedral built over a former mosque, with a striking rose window and quiet cloister.
The elegant main square lined with noble palaces and an obelisk commemorating the 1558 Turkish siege, perfect for evening people-watching.
A narrow, colorful harbor lined with fishing boats, seafood restaurants, and bars carved into the rock below the old town.
An 18th-century watchtower at the harbor mouth offering sunset views over the sea toward Mallorca.
One of Europe's oldest roofed structures, this Bronze Age megalithic burial chamber sits in open countryside just outside town.
A dramatic natural rock arch and sea cove a short drive from town, popular for swimming and photos.
Palau Salort occasionally opens its doors, revealing the aristocratic interiors of Ciutadella's 19th-century elite.

activities.text: Relax at Ses Fonts de Sant Joan
activities.detail: A green, spring-fed park just outside the old walls, ideal for a shaded walk away from summer heat.

food.detail: Menorca's signature lobster stew, rich with tomato and slow-cooked broth, best enjoyed at a port-side restaurant.
A tangy, aged cow's milk cheese with DOP status, often served with local olives and bread.
Cured, spreadable pork sausage flavored with paprika, traditionally drizzled with honey on toasted bread.
The island's beloved gin-and-lemonade cocktail, born from centuries of British-influenced gin distilling in Menorca.
A light tomato-and-garlic broth traditionally paired with fresh figs, a rustic summer classic.
A soft, coiled pastry dusted with sugar, perfect for breakfast with coffee in a plaza café.

culture.text: Time visits around Sant Joan festivities
culture.detail: Late June's Fiestas de Sant Joan feature dramatic horse rearing (jaleo) through packed streets, a centuries-old tradition unique to Ciutadella.
Many shops and some restaurants close for a few hours in the early afternoon, especially outside peak tourist season.
As an active place of worship, modest attire is appreciated when entering Santa Maria Cathedral.

culture.text: Navigate the old town on foot
culture.detail: Streets in the historic core are narrow and largely pedestrian, so it's best explored slowly rather than by car.

places.name: Ibiza Town
