guide/festivals.json.title: Festivals & events
guide/festivals.json.subtitle: 42 celebrations through the year — pick a day to see what's on.

festivals.name: New Year's Day
festivals.description: Spain's official start to the new year, celebrated nationwide with fireworks, family gatherings, and the tradition of eating twelve grapes at midnight.

festivals.name: Epiphany / Día de Reyes
festivals.description: Spain's most important gift-giving holiday honoring the Three Kings, featuring grand parades and the traditional Roscón de Reyes cake.

festivals.name: Seville Carnival
festivals.description: One of Spain's most vibrant carnivals with elaborate costumes, satirical groups (comparsas), and days of dancing and celebration throughout the city.

festivals.name: Tenerife Carnival
festivals.description: The second-largest carnival in the world with colorful parades, costumes, music, and street performances rivaling Rio de Janeiro.

festivals.name: Carnival of Cádiz
festivals.description: A historic carnival known for its witty satirical groups (comparsas) and chirigotas that poke fun at current events and politics.

festivals.name: Las Fallas
festivals.description: A spectacular festival featuring enormous satirical papier-mâché sculptures (fallas) that are set on fire in the climactic Nit del Foc, with parades and fireworks throughout.

festivals.name: Good Friday / Viernes Santo
festivals.description: A national public holiday marked by solemn religious processions, especially dramatic in Andalusian cities like Seville with centuries-old traditions.

festivals.name: Easter Monday
festivals.description: A national public holiday celebrated throughout Spain with family gatherings, church services, and traditional Easter sweets.

festivals.name: San Jorge Festival
festivals.description: Catalonia's celebration of Sant Jordi with a tradition of exchanging roses and books, filling Barcelona's streets with color and literary spirit.

festivals.name: Labor Day
festivals.description: Spain's national public holiday honoring workers with parades, demonstrations, and celebrations across the country.

festivals.name: Festival of San Isidro
festivals.description: Madrid's patron saint festival featuring bullfights, concerts, theater, and street parties throughout the capital.

festivals.name: Romería de El Rocío
festivals.description: A massive pilgrimage of nearly a million people traveling on foot, horse, and cart to the Doñana wetlands to honor the Virgin of El Rocío.

festivals.name: Sónar Barcelona
festivals.description: One of Europe's premier electronic music and multimedia festivals attracting top DJs and artists from around the world.

festivals.name: Festa de Sant Joan
festivals.description: Catalonia's midsummer celebration with bonfires, fireworks, and the traditional Coca de Recapte pastry enjoyed throughout the region.

festivals.name: Bonfires of San Juan
festivals.description: A Valencian tradition featuring bonfires on beaches, parades, and street parties celebrating midsummer with fireworks and revelry.

festivals.name: Assumption of Mary
festivals.description: A major national public holiday marking the Assumption, celebrated with religious services and summer holidays throughout Spain.

festivals.name: Running of the Bulls (San Fermín)
festivals.description: Pamplona's world-famous festival featuring the iconic encierro where bulls chase participants through streets, plus bullfights, concerts, and parades.

festivals.name: Vitoria Festival
festivals.description: Vitoria's week-long celebration featuring concerts, theater, street performances, and Basque cultural displays with the famous white and red costume tradition.

festivals.name: Moros y Cristianos
festivals.description: A colorful festival reenacting the historical battles between Moors and Christians with elaborate parades, costumes, and fireworks.

festivals.name: Bilbao BBK Live
festivals.description: A major international music festival featuring rock, indie, electronic, and hip-hop artists across multiple stages in the Basque countryside.

festivals.name: Benicàssim Festival
festivals.description: A famous Mediterranean music festival attracting international indie and electronic artists with a beach setting.

festivals.name: National Day / Día Nacional de España
festivals.description: Spain's national holiday celebrating the founding of the Spanish nation with military parades, flag ceremonies, and official celebrations throughout the country.

festivals.name: La Tomatina
festivals.description: A world-renowned festival where participants throw overripe tomatoes at each other in a massive food fight that turns streets red.

festivals.name: Aste Nagusia (Big Week)
festivals.description: Bilbao's grand week-long celebration featuring concerts, traditional Basque sports (txuleta), gastronomic events, and fireworks.

festivals.name: Veranos de la Villa
festivals.description: Madrid's summer arts festival offering free and paid theater, music, dance, and cinema events throughout the city.

festivals.name: Diada de Catalunya
festivals.description: Catalonia's national holiday commemorating the 1714 siege with political demonstrations, parades, and displays of regional pride.

festivals.name: Oyster Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of Galician seafood culture featuring oyster tastings, traditional music, and culinary demonstrations.

festivals.name: Halloween/Samhain Celebrations
festivals.description: Increasingly celebrated in Spain with themed parties, costume events, and street celebrations, particularly in major cities.

festivals.name: All Saints' Day
festivals.description: A national public holiday honoring the dead with visits to cemeteries, traditional all-saints bread, and family gatherings.

festivals.name: Castanyada
festivals.description: A Catalan tradition on All Saints' Day involving roasted chestnuts and sweet potato (boniato) shared with family and friends.

festivals.name: Constitution Day
festivals.description: Spain's national holiday celebrating the 1978 Constitution with official ceremonies, parades, and flag displays throughout the country.

festivals.name: Christmas Markets and Fairs
festivals.description: Holiday markets selling traditional crafts, toys, and decorations throughout Spain's cities and towns during the festive season.

festivals.name: Christmas Day
festivals.description: Spain's national public holiday celebrated with family gatherings, church services, and traditional festive meals.

festivals.name: Lucía Festival
festivals.description: A pre-Christmas celebration in Asturias honoring Saint Lucy with traditional music, dancing, and local festivities.

festivals.name: Fire Festival (Iruak)
festivals.description: A winter solstice celebration in the Basque Country with bonfires, traditional rituals, and community gatherings.

festivals.name: Nochevieja (New Year's Eve)
festivals.description: Spain's celebrated New Year's Eve with the famous grape-eating tradition at Puerta del Sol in Madrid and celebrations throughout the country.

festivals.name: Carnival of Orihuela
festivals.description: A Valencian carnival featuring satirical parades, colorful costumes, and the burning of effigies (judas) in traditional style.

festivals.name: Andalucía Day
festivals.description: Andalusia's regional holiday celebrating the region's autonomy with official ceremonies, concerts, and cultural performances.

festivals.name: Melilla Autonomous City Day
festivals.description: Melilla's celebration of its autonomous status with parades, cultural events, and regional festivities.

festivals.name: Feria de Abril
festivals.description: Seville's spectacular spring fair featuring traditional Andalusian dress, flamenco dancing, horse parades, and aristocratic family pavilions.

festivals.name: Mercat Medieval de Montblanc
festivals.description: A medieval market festival recreating life in the Middle Ages with craftspeople, entertainers, and authentic period activities.

festivals.name: Festa Major de Gràcia
festivals.description: Barcelona's neighborhood festival celebrating Gràcia with decorated streets, outdoor concerts, performances, and parades.

guide/history.json.title: A Short History of Spain
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 14 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Paleolithic Iberia
items.summary: Hunter-gatherers roamed the peninsula, leaving behind some of Europe's oldest cave art.
items.text: The first humans arrived on the Iberian Peninsula during the Lower Paleolithic, following herds of megafauna across a landscape of ice ages and mild periods. By the Upper Paleolithic, sophisticated hunter-gatherers had established themselves throughout the region, developing increasingly complex tools and social structures. The cave paintings of Altamira, created around 36,000 years ago, stand among the world's earliest artistic masterpieces, depicting bison, horses, and hand stencils with remarkable skill and naturalism. These early peoples survived through hunting, gathering, and fishing, adapting to dramatic climate changes over tens of thousands of years.

items.label: Neolithic and Bronze Age Settlement
items.summary: Agriculture arrived from the Mediterranean, transforming scattered hunter-gatherers into settled farming communities.
items.text: The Neolithic revolution came late to Iberia but profoundly reshaped society, with farming communities gradually replacing hunter-gatherers from around 5,000 BC onwards. Megalithic monuments, like dolmens and stone circles, dotted the landscape as communities developed hierarchies and collective burial practices. The Bronze Age brought technological advances and increased trade networks connecting Iberia to Mediterranean civilizations, with coastal regions like Tartessos developing sophisticated societies and metalworking skills. By the second millennium BC, distinct cultural groups had emerged across the peninsula, from Celtic peoples in the north to Lusitanians in the west and Celtiberians in the interior.

items.label: Iron Age and Ancient Peoples
items.summary: Celts, Lusitanians, and Celtiberians established independent kingdoms while Phoenicians and Greeks colonized the coasts.
items.text: Iron Age Iberia was a mosaic of fiercely independent tribal societies, each with distinct languages, customs, and territorial claims that resisted outside domination. The Lusitanians in the west, the Celtiberians in the central plateau, and various Celtic groups in the north proved formidable warriors, renowned throughout the Mediterranean for their courage and martial skill. Phoenician traders from the east established colonies like Gadir (modern Cádiz) and developed extensive commerce networks, while Greeks founded settlements on the eastern coast. These indigenous peoples created rich cultures of art, metalwork, and oral tradition, but their fragmentation would prove their greatest vulnerability when facing unified external powers.

items.label: Roman Conquest and Hispania
items.summary: Rome conquered the peninsula in two centuries of brutal warfare, transforming it into the wealthy province of Hispania.
items.text: The Second Punic War brought Roman legions to Iberia in 218 BC, initiating a conquest that would take nearly two hundred years to complete and fundamentally reshape the peninsula. The most fierce resistance came from the Lusitanians under Viriathus and the Celtiberians at Numantia, whose sieges became legendary for their courage and tragic defiance, yet Rome's superior resources and organization proved unstoppable. Under Roman rule, Hispania became one of the empire's most prosperous provinces, with roads, aqueducts, cities, and a unified legal system bringing unprecedented development and stability. Latin language, Roman law, and eventually Christianity transformed Iberian culture so completely that its Celtic and Lusitanian heritage faded into legend, while the peninsula became thoroughly integrated into Mediterranean civilization.

items.label: Visigothic Kingdom
items.summary: Germanic Visigoths inherited Rome's collapsed provinces and established a Christian kingdom that would last two and a half centuries.
items.text: As the Western Roman Empire crumbled in the fifth century, the Visigoths, a Germanic people who had been settling in Iberia as Roman foederati, established their own kingdom with its capital at Toledo. The Visigothic realm adopted Roman administrative structures, Latin language, and Arian then Catholic Christianity, creating a synthesis of Germanic and Mediterranean culture that maintained continuity with the Roman past. Though often portrayed as dark ages, Visigothic Spain was actually a period of vibrant cultural activity, with King Isidore of Seville producing the encyclopedic Etymologiae and courts fostering learning and artistic production. Internal political instability, factionalism between rival noble families, and economic strain plagued the kingdom's later centuries, creating vulnerabilities that would have fateful consequences for the entire peninsula.

items.label: Islamic Conquest and Al-Andalus
items.summary: Muslim armies from North Africa conquered most of the peninsula in seven years, establishing Al-Andalus, an Islamic civilization that would endure nearly eight centuries.
items.text: In 711, Berber and Arab forces crossed from North Africa and rapidly overwhelmed the fragmented Visigothic kingdom in a series of decisive battles, establishing the Al-Andalus emirate that would become one of medieval Europe's most advanced civilizations. At its peak in the tenth century under the Umayyad Caliphate of Córdoba, Al-Andalus was a beacon of learning, with scholars advancing mathematics, astronomy, medicine, and philosophy while Christian Europe struggled through its early medieval period. The great cities of Córdoba, Granada, Seville, and Toledo became centers of remarkable intellectual and artistic achievement, where Muslims, Christians, and Jews coexisted in a complex society of cultural exchange, though marked by shifting hierarchies and periodic conflict. Despite its sophistication and cultural brilliance, Al-Andalus was perpetually fragmented into competing taifas or city-states, its wealth constantly draining away to pay tribute to Christian kingdoms while facing pressure from North African dynasties like the Almoravids and Almohads.

items.label: The Reconquista
items.summary: Christian kingdoms gradually reconquered the peninsula through seven centuries of warfare, crusade, and political consolidation.
items.text: The Christian Reconquista began almost immediately after the Islamic conquest, with small Christian principalities in the north—particularly Asturias, León, Castile, Aragon, and Catalonia—slowly pushing southward through centuries of warfare, marriage alliances, and political maneuvering. This long struggle shaped Christian Iberian identity, producing a militaristic culture of frontier kingdoms locked in perpetual conflict, where knights and nobles accumulated power and land through reconquest and where the Church became deeply embedded in politics and warfare. The major turning point came at the Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa in 1212, which shattered Muslim military power and accelerated Christian advancement, leading to the conquest of Córdoba, Seville, and eventually Granada. By the late fifteenth century, only the Nasrid Kingdom of Granada remained in Muslim hands, squeezed into the southeastern corner of the peninsula, surviving through tribute and vassalage until the final conquest in 1492.

items.label: The Catholic Monarchs and Spanish Empire
items.summary: Ferdinand and Isabella unified Spain, conquered Granada, sponsored Columbus, and built Europe's first global empire.
items.text: The marriage of Ferdinand of Aragon and Isabella of Castile in 1469 created a powerful partnership that would transform the peninsula and the world; their conquest of Granada in 1492 completed the Reconquista and their sponsorship of Columbus opened the Atlantic age. This same year, they expelled Spain's Jewish population and intensified persecution of Muslims, establishing the Spanish Inquisition and beginning a process of religious homogenization through violence and forced conversion. The Spanish empire that followed became history's first truly global power, accumulating vast territories in the Americas, Italy, the Low Countries, and elsewhere under the Hapsburg dynasty, making Spain the dominant force in European politics for the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries. However, this empire's immense wealth and power came with devastating costs: endless wars, religious rigidity, economic stagnation in the mother country, and the brutal exploitation of indigenous peoples across the Atlantic, all of which would gradually erode Spanish vitality.

items.label: The Bourbon Reformation and Decline
items.summary: The French Bourbon dynasty took Spain's throne and modernized the kingdom's administration, but Spain continued declining as a European power.
items.text: The War of Spanish Succession brought the French House of Bourbon to Spain's throne in 1700, replacing the failing Hapsburgs and initiating a period of administrative reform and centralization that strengthened the monarchy but failed to revive Spanish prosperity. Enlightenment-influenced ministers like the Count of Aranda and the Duke of Alba attempted to modernize Spanish institutions, commerce, and education while asserting state control over the Church, yet these reforms faced fierce resistance from the nobility and clergy. Throughout the eighteenth century, Spain lost global influence and territory, ceding Gibraltar to Britain, losing colonies to British conquest, and finding itself dependent on French military support and diplomatic guidance. The American wars of independence in the 1820s would cost Spain its greatest remaining treasure, and by 1800 Spain was clearly a second-rate power, financially exhausted and culturally isolated by its religious orthodoxy and resistance to modernity.

items.label: The Peninsular War and Liberal Spain
items.summary: French occupation sparked a devastating war, then constitutional liberals and conservatives battled for Spain's future in a century of instability.
items.text: Napoleon's 1808 occupation of Spain to enforce his Continental System sparked the Peninsular War, a six-year conflict combining regular armies, guerrilla resistance, and brutal reprisals that cost Spain hundreds of thousands of lives and ravaged its economy. Out of this chaos emerged Spanish liberalism, with the Cádiz Cortes of 1812 producing one of Europe's most advanced constitutions, establishing parliamentary government and limiting royal power—a revolutionary document that alarmed European monarchies and influenced liberal movements across the continent. The restoration of Ferdinand VII initiated decades of bitter conflict between liberal and conservative forces, with Spain experiencing multiple constitutions, military coups, and civil wars as different visions of the nation clashed. This era of instability culminated in the First Spanish Republic (1873-1874), a brief, chaotic experiment that collapsed into monarchy restoration, leaving Spain exhausted, divided, and unable to resolve its fundamental divisions through legitimate political processes.

items.label: The Restoration and Regional Tensions
items.summary: A restored constitutional monarchy brought surface stability while Catalonia and the Basque Country awakened to nationalist demands and labor radicalism spread.
items.text: The Restoration of 1875 established the Bourbon Restoration Monarchy, creating a stable if corrupt two-party system that rotated power between Conservatives and Liberals through manipulation and electoral fraud, a system known as turno pacifico. This stability proved superficial, concealing surging regional nationalism, particularly in Catalonia where industrialization created a prosperous bourgeoisie seeking cultural and political autonomy, and in the Basque Country where distinct identity and ETA nationalism emerged. Industrial growth in Catalonia and the Basque Country created militant labor movements and anarchist movements that terrified the establishment, leading to waves of strikes, assassinations, and brutal repression, turning Spanish cities into battlegrounds of class conflict. The loss of the last major colonies in 1898—the Spanish-American War—dealt a psychological blow to Spanish elites, generating the philosophical pessimism of the Generation of '98 intellectuals while military disasters in Morocco throughout the early twentieth century revealed the weakness of Spanish power.

items.label: Dictatorship and the Second Republic
items.summary: A military coup established dictatorship, then democratic revolution created the Second Republic before Civil War shattered both visions of Spain.
items.text: General Miguel Primo de Rivera's 1923 military coup promised order and efficiency but delivered authoritarian government that proved incapable of resolving Spain's fundamental conflicts, ultimately discrediting the Restoration system and the monarchy itself. His fall in 1930 led to the Second Spanish Republic in 1931, a democratic experiment that initially inspired hope with its progressive constitution, regional autonomy, land reform, and secular policies, attracting intellectuals and workers to its vision of modern Spain. However, the Republic faced implacable enemies: the military, landowners, and the Church viewed it as an existential threat, while radical leftists and anarchists felt it didn't go far enough in transforming Spanish society, leaving the moderate center with no solid support. The 1936 military rebellion of General Francisco Franco split Spain in two and launched a catastrophic three-year Civil War that killed nearly a million people, attracted international fascist and communist intervention, and ended with Franco's total victory and dictatorship.

items.label: Franco's Dictatorship and Recovery
items.summary: Franco's authoritarian regime ruled Spain with an iron fist for 36 years, gradually modernizing the economy while suppressing freedoms and regional identities.
items.text: Franco's victory in the Civil War initiated one of Western Europe's longest dictatorships, characterized by political repression, censorship, suppression of regional languages and cultures, and execution or imprisonment of political opponents throughout the 1940s and 1950s. The early Franco years were marked by economic isolation and stagnation as the regime pursued autarky, but international isolation began lifting after 1950 as Cold War anticommunism made Franco palatable to the West, leading to NATO membership and American military bases in exchange for geostrategic cooperation. Beginning in the late 1950s and accelerating through the 1960s, Franco's Spain underwent rapid industrialization and economic modernization, driven by tourism, foreign investment, and labor-intensive manufacturing, creating a growing middle class and prosperous cities while rural areas stagnated. Yet despite economic progress, Franco's political system remained frozen: no parties, no elections, no freedoms, with the secret police still active and regional nationalism suppressed, creating growing tensions between the prosperous, modern economy and the archaic, authoritarian political system.

items.label: Democracy and Modern Spain
items.summary: After Franco's death, Spain transitioned peacefully to democracy, joined Europe, and became a modern, prosperous nation despite persistent regional conflicts.
items.text: Franco's death in 1975 initiated one of history's most successful transitions to democracy, as King Juan Carlos I, contrary to many expectations, shepherded Spain toward democratic reform through careful negotiation with reformist elites, resulting in the 1978 Constitution that established a parliamentary monarchy and decentralized regional autonomy. The 1980s and 1990s saw Spain integrate fully into Western Europe through European Union membership, NATO participation, and adoption of European standards and values, transforming Spanish education, culture, and institutions while enjoying rapid economic growth and rising living standards. Spain's regions, particularly Catalonia and the Basque Country, gained substantial autonomy and self-government, allowing regional languages and cultures to flourish, yet independence movements only grew stronger as prosperity and self-government intensified regional identity and demands for complete sovereignty. Today Spain stands as a modern, economically advanced European democracy with vibrant culture and technology sectors, yet it faces persistent challenges: economic crises and inequality, the Catalan independence movement that threatens natio

sections.title: Regional Cuisines: A Land of Culinary Diversity
sections.body: Spain's cuisine varies dramatically by region, shaped by geography and history. The Basque Country is renowned for pintxos and refined seafood cooking, while Galicia excels in octopus and shellfish thanks to its Atlantic coastline. Andalusia offers fried fish, cold soups, and Moorish-influenced flavors, and Catalonia blends French and Mediterranean techniques with rustic mountain fare.

sections.title: Signature Dishes You Must Try
sections.body: Paella, originally from Valencia, showcases rice cooked with saffron, vegetables, and meat or seafood. Jamón ibérico, cured for months from acorn-fed pigs, is a national treasure enjoyed thinly sliced. Tortilla española, a simple potato and egg omelette, appears everywhere from bars to home kitchens, and gazpacho offers a refreshing cold tomato soup perfect for hot summers.

sections.title: Tapas Culture and Small Plates
sections.body: Tapas are small, shareable dishes meant to accompany drinks and encourage socializing rather than solitary dining. Bars often serve free tapas with a drink order in cities like Granada, while elsewhere you order dishes like croquetas, patatas bravas, or boquerones individually. This style of eating encourages hopping between bars, sampling different specialties at each stop.

sections.title: Spanish Wines: From Rioja to Cava
sections.body: Rioja, Spain's most famous wine region, produces excellent reds from Tempranillo grapes, aged in oak for distinctive complexity. Ribera del Duero offers bold, structured reds, while Catalonia's Penedès region is celebrated for Cava, Spain's sparkling wine made using the traditional method. Sherry from Jerez, ranging from bone-dry Fino to sweet Pedro Ximénez, remains an underappreciated treasure worth exploring.

sections.title: Dining Customs and Meal Timing
sections.body: Spanish meal times run later than much of Europe, with lunch often starting at 2pm and dinner rarely before 9pm or 10pm. The midday meal is traditionally the largest, sometimes accompanied by a post-lunch siesta in smaller towns. Evening socializing frequently begins with tapas and drinks before a late dinner, reflecting the relaxed, communal pace of Spanish dining culture.

sections.title: Markets and Local Ingredients
sections.body: Vibrant markets like Madrid's Mercado de San Miguel or Barcelona's La Boqueria showcase Spain's obsession with fresh, quality ingredients. Olive oil, produced abundantly in Andalusia, forms the backbone of Spanish cooking, while regional cheeses like Manchego add distinctive character to meals. Seasonal produce and local sourcing remain central to Spanish culinary identity, even in modern restaurant settings.

sections.title: Sweet Endings: Spanish Desserts
sections.body: Churros with thick hot chocolate make a beloved breakfast or late-night treat, especially in Madrid. Turrón, a nougat confection with almonds and honey, is traditionally associated with Christmas but enjoyed year-round. Crema catalana, similar to crème brûlée, and flan showcase Spain's love of custard-based desserts with a caramelized touch.

guide/transport.json.title: Getting around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads and how to move around Spain

sections.title: Trains
sections.body: Spain's high-speed AVE network connects major cities like Madrid, Barcelona, Seville, and Valencia quickly and comfortably, often rivaling flights for convenience once you factor in city-center-to-city-center travel. Renfe operates the national rail system, and booking in advance usually secures cheaper fares. Regional trains (Media Distancia and Cercanías) are useful for shorter hops and commuter routes, though slower and less frequent than the AVE.

sections.title: Driving
sections.body: Renting a car is ideal for exploring rural areas, small villages, and regions like Andalusia or the Basque Country where public transport is sparse. Spain's toll and free highways are well-maintained, and road signage is clear, though city driving and parking can be stressful in places like Madrid or Barcelona. An International Driving Permit is recommended alongside your home license, and note that manual transmission cars are the norm unless you request automatic in advance.

sections.title: Flights
sections.body: Domestic flights are practical for covering long distances quickly, such as traveling from Madrid to the Canary or Balearic Islands. Budget airlines like Vueling, Ryanair, and Iberia Express offer frequent routes between major cities and islands at competitive prices. For mainland travel, however, trains are often more efficient once airport transfer times and check-in procedures are factored in.

sections.title: City transport
sections.body: Madrid and Barcelona boast extensive metro systems that are affordable, efficient, and easy to navigate with multilingual signage. Buses supplement metro service and are especially useful in smaller cities without rail networks, while taxis and ride-share apps like Cabify and Uber are widely available. Many cities also offer bike-share programs and increasingly pedestrian-friendly city centers, making walking a pleasant option for short distances.

sections.title: Practical tips
sections.body: Purchase train and flight tickets in advance for the best prices, especially during peak travel seasons like summer and Semana Santa. Validate any physical tickets before boarding regional trains or buses to avoid fines, and download the Renfe or Moovit apps for real-time schedules and route planning. Keep in mind that many businesses and some transport services observe a midday lull, so plan connections accordingly, particularly in smaller towns.
